一个民间传说认为，1593年10月，西班牙帝国的一名士兵(1908年版的名字是吉尔·佩雷斯)被神秘地从菲律宾的马尼拉运送到墨西哥城的广场市长(现在的佐卡洛)。墨西哥人不相信这名士兵自称来自菲律宾的说法，直到几个月后，他关于戈麦斯·佩雷斯·达斯马里尼亚斯(Gómez Pérez Dasmariñas)遇刺的说法得到了一艘带着这一消息横渡太平洋的船只乘客的证实。民俗学家托马斯·阿里伯恩·詹维尔(Thomas Allibone Janvier)在1908年将这一传说描述为目前在墨西哥城所有阶层人口中流行的传说。[1]二十世纪相信这个故事的超自然调查人员提供了心灵传送和外星人绑架作为解释。
1593年10月24日，这名士兵在菲律宾总司令的马尼拉守卫着戈伯纳多宫殿(Palacio Del Gobernado)。前一天晚上，州长戈麦斯·佩雷斯·达斯马里尼亚斯(Gómez Pérez Dasmariñas)被中国海盗暗杀，但警卫仍然守卫着宫殿，等待新州长的任命。士兵开始感到头晕目眩，筋疲力尽。他靠在墙上，闭着眼睛休息了一会儿。
几秒钟后，当他睁开眼睛时，他发现自己身处墨西哥总督的墨西哥城，横跨数千公里的海洋。一些警卫发现他穿错了制服，并开始质疑他是谁。墨西哥城的人们还不知道菲律宾总督被暗杀的消息。据报道，这名被运送的士兵在马尼拉穿着宫廷警卫的制服，并知道自己的死讯。(事实上，佩雷斯·达斯马里尼亚斯是在距离马尼拉一段距离的海上遇难的。)。
当局以逃兵和魔鬼仆人的罪名将他送进监狱。几个月后，州长去世的消息从菲律宾的一艘大帆船上传到了墨西哥。其中一名乘客认出了这名被监禁的士兵，并说他在总督去世后的第二天在菲律宾见过他。他最终被当局从监狱释放，并被允许回家。
住在墨西哥的美国民俗学家托马斯·阿利伯恩·詹维尔(Thomas Allibone Janvier)在1908年12月版的“哈珀杂志”(Harper‘s Magazine)上以“活着的幽灵传奇”(Legend Of The Living Spectre)的名义讲述了这个故事，并将这名士兵命名为吉尔·佩雷斯(Gil Pérez)。这个故事是1910年出版的名为墨西哥城传奇的系列丛书中的一个。Janvier指出，类似的主题在民俗中很常见。[2]华盛顿·欧文的“阿尔罕布拉的故事”包含了州长曼科和士兵的故事，与传说有相似之处。[2][3]。
Janvier&#39；的1908年账户是基于墨西哥民俗学家路易斯·冈萨雷斯·奥布雷贡(Luis González Obregón)的西班牙语版本，发表在他1900年的文集“México Viejo：Notisas históricas，tradiciones，leyendas y viumbres”(&#34；老墨西哥：历史笔记，民间传说，传说和习俗&34；)下标题&#34；un Aparecido&34；(&#34；一个幻影&34；)。Obregón将这个故事追溯到1698年弗雷·加斯帕·德·圣奥古斯丁(Fray Gaspar de San Agustín)对西班牙征服菲律宾的描述，其中讲述了这个故事是事实；圣奥古斯丁没有提到这名士兵的名字，并将他的运输归因于巫术。[4]。
Janvier说，奥布雷贡断言，在1609年，安东尼奥·德·莫尔加(Antonio De Morga)曾写道，佩雷斯·达斯马里尼亚斯(Pérez Dasmariñas)于同一天在墨西哥被发现死亡，尽管德·莫尔加表示不知道这是如何发生的。[5][6]何塞·里萨尔(JoséRizal)提到了当时西班牙菲律宾的许多其他奇迹故事；[4]路易斯·韦克曼(Luis Weckmann)对西班牙的墨西哥也提出了同样的观点。[7]奥布雷贡的继任者阿特米奥·德瓦莱·亚利兹佩(Artemio De Valle Arizpe)1936年的合集《生者与死者的故事》(Historias De Vivos Y Muertos)包括了这个故事的一个版本，标题为#34；Por el aire vino，Por la mar se fue&34；(他是乘飞机来的，他是乘海路离开的)。
几位作家对这个故事给出了超自然的解释。[8]莫里斯·K·杰瑟普[9]和第八任克兰卡蒂伯爵布林斯利·勒波尔·特伦奇[10]建议外星人绑架，而科林·威尔逊[11]和加里·布莱克伍德[12]则建议进行心灵传送。
安东尼奥·德·莫尔加(1890)[1609]。Rizal，José(编辑)。Las Islas Filipinas Sucesos de las Filipinas(西班牙语)。巴黎：Garnier Hermanos。第31-36页。
德·圣奥古斯丁，加斯帕(1698)。12：de los Embaxadore que llegaronàManila，enviados por el Emperado del Japan，y del Rey de Camboxa，y del lastimoso fin del总督戈麦斯·佩雷斯·达斯马里尼亚斯(Gomez Perez Dasmariñas)。费利皮纳斯岛上的征服者(西班牙语)。马德里：曼努埃尔·鲁伊斯·德·穆尔加。第465页。
冈萨雷斯·奥布雷贡，路易斯(1900年)。联合国阿帕雷西多(PDF)。México Viejo：Notisas históricas，tradiciones，leyendas y customumres(西班牙语)。巴黎；墨西哥城：Librería de la VDA。德·C·布雷。第181-185页。奥布雷贡，路易斯·冈萨雷斯(1937年)。墨西哥的街道。作者声明：Blanche Collet Wagner；旧金山：乔治·菲尔兹。

^Ocampo，Ambeth R.(1993)。阿吉纳尔多的早餐和更多回顾散文。铁砧出版公司。第8-9页，ISBN：9789712702815。

note_en:
Jump to navigation  Jump to search  A  folk legend holds that in October 1593 a soldier of the  Spanish Empire (named  Gil Pérez in a 1908 version) was mysteriously transported from  Manila in the  Philippines to the Plaza Mayor (now the  Zócalo) in  Mexico City. The soldier's claim to have come from the Philippines was disbelieved by the Mexicans until his account of the assassination of  Gómez Pérez Dasmariñas was corroborated months later by the passengers of a ship which had crossed the  Pacific Ocean with the news. Folklorist  Thomas Allibone Janvier in 1908 described the legend as "current among all classes of the population of the City of Mexico".  [1] Twentieth-century  paranormal investigators giving credence to the story have offered  teleportation and  alien abduction as explanations.
   On October 24, 1593, the soldier was doing his job guarding the Palacio del Gobernador in  Manila in the  Captaincy General of the Philippines. The night before, Governor  Gómez Pérez Dasmariñas was assassinated by Chinese pirates, but the guards still guarded the palace and awaited the appointment of a new governor. The soldier began to feel dizzy and exhausted. He leaned against the wall and rested for a moment with his eyes closed.
 When he opened his eyes a few seconds later, he found himself in Mexico City, in the  Viceroyalty of Mexico, thousands of kilometres across the ocean. Some guards found him in the wrong uniform and began to question who he was. The news of the assassination of the Governor of the Philippines was still unknown to the people in Mexico City. The transported soldier was reportedly wearing the palace guards' uniform in Manila and knew of his death. (In fact, Pérez Dasmariñas was killed at sea some distance from Manila.)
 The authorities placed him in jail for being a deserter and with charges of being a servant of the devil. Months later, news of the governor's death came to Mexico on a galleon from the Philippines. One of the passengers recognized the imprisoned soldier and said that he had seen him in the Philippines a day after the death of the Governor. He was eventually released from jail by the authorities and allowed to go back home.
  Thomas Allibone Janvier, an American folklorist living in Mexico, recounted the story as  Legend of the Living Spectre in the December 1908 edition of   Harper's Magazine, naming the soldier as Gil Pérez. The story was one of a series entitled  Legends of the City of Mexico published in a collected volume in 1910. Janvier notes that similar motifs are common in folklore.  [2]  Washington Irving's   Tales of the Alhambra includes the story "Governor Manco and the Soldier", which bears similarities to the legend.  [2]  [3]
 Janvier's 1908 account was based on a Spanish version by Mexican folklorist  Luis González Obregón  [ es], published in his 1900 collection  México viejo: noticias históricas, tradiciones, leyendas y costumbres ("Old Mexico: historical notes, folklore, legends and customs") under the title  "Un aparecido" ("An  apparition"). Obregón traces the story to a 1698 account by Fray  Gaspar de San Agustín  [ es] of the  Spanish conquest of the Philippines, which recounts the story as fact; San Agustin does not name the soldier and ascribed his transportation to  witchcraft.  [4]
 Janvier says Obregón asserts that in 1609,  Antonio de Morga had written that Pérez Dasmariñas' death was known in Mexico the same day, though de Morga expresses ignorance of how this came to be.  [5]  [6]  José Rizal notes many other miraculous stories from the Spanish Philippines of the time;  [4] Luis Weckmann makes the same point in relation to Spanish Mexico.  [7] A 1936 collection,  Historias de vivos y muertos ("Stories of the living and the dead") by Obregón's successor  Artemio de Valle Arizpe  [ es], included a version of the story entitled  "Por el aire vino, por la mar se fue" ("He came by air, he left by sea").
 Several writers have offered  paranormal explanations for the story.  [8]  Morris K. Jessup  [9] and  Brinsley Le Poer Trench, 8th Earl of Clancarty,  [10] suggested  alien abduction, while  Colin Wilson  [11] and  Gary Blackwood  [12] suggested  teleportation.

Bouret. pp. 181–185 .   Obregón, Luis González (1937).  The Streets of Mexico. Translated by Blanche Collet Wagner. San Francisco: George Fields.

title: 1593运来的士兵传奇

note:
作为选民，作为记者，作为公民，作为作家，作为曾经被认为是安全民主的参与者，我们今天生活在美国，通过玛莎·格森(跟随Bálint Magyar)。
让这些话深入人心：专制的尝试…。“为真正行使独裁权力做准备。”
当一位获得权力的政治人物试图利用这一权力来探索建立独裁国家的可能性时，这是一种独裁尝试。这个过程的其他阶段是“专制突破”和“专制巩固”。我希望我们永远不会谈到这两点。但我不能肯定地说我们不会。玛莎·格森也不能。(阅读《独裁统治下的幸存》。)。
我建议大型国家新闻编辑部创建威胁建模团队，帮助组织对特朗普政府和2020年选举的报道。我担心的是，美国的民主正处于危险之中。传统的竞选报道无法应对这种威胁。
我咨询了两个在其他危险区域从事过威胁建模工作的人，我请他们帮助我想象一下它在新闻编辑室设置中的可能用途。
其中一人目前是乔治敦大学的法学客座教授。2015年至2017年，他担任国家安全委员会(National Security Council)工作人员反恐高级主任，在那里他参加了所谓的“桌面”演习，试图想象威胁将如何发展。(这通常被称为“威胁思维”。)。作为一名为行政部门从事反恐工作的“客户”，他使用了以情报机构和执法部门为基础的威胁建模团队的产品。关于唐纳德·特朗普对2020年大选的攻击。
我的另一个线人是Facebook的前首席安全官和雅虎的首席信息安全官，他将雅虎描述为“一家公司最高级别的人，他的任务是保护公司的系统、软件和其他技术，把我从盖尔策和斯塔莫斯那里学到的东西结合起来--以及我最近参加的阿斯彭研究所(Aspen Institute)关于选举威胁的非正式简报--以下是我认为它是如何运作的。
建议：大型全国性新闻提供商-美国广播公司(ABC)、哥伦比亚广播公司(CBS)、全国广播公司(NBC)、CNN、NPR、PBS、美联社(AP)、路透社(Reuters)、纽约时报(New York Times)、华盛顿邮报(Washington Post)-应该有威胁建模团队，就像他们都有民意调查人员一样。这些团队将试图找出自由公正的选举和美国民主面临的最严重威胁，以便他们的新闻编辑部能够采取适当的行动。
Geltzer说：“威胁建模是以使我们能够准备预防、减轻或应对危险的方式来阐明危险的努力。”“这是一种识别和描述威胁的方式，有助于我们解决这些威胁。”其目的是以更系统的方式评估漏洞和预测攻击，而不仅仅是担心它们。
斯塔莫斯是这么说的。“威胁建模”是一个正式的过程，通过该过程，团队绘制出系统的潜在对手和这些对手的能力，绘制系统的攻击面和这些攻击面中的潜在漏洞，然后将这两个集合匹配在一起，以构建可能的漏洞和攻击的模型。
盖尔策告诉我：“你首先需要对你想要保护的东西有深刻的认识。”(我稍后会再谈到这一点；这一点很关键。)。你还需要在可能出现的各种危险方面的专业知识。对于特朗普来说，这是对美国民主的威胁，他们对“民主是如何消亡的”进行了认真的研究。
对于其他类型的威胁，新闻编辑部通常拥有内部专业知识，比如NBC的布兰迪·扎德罗兹尼(Brandy Zadrozny)和本·柯林斯(Ben Collins)，他们对自己的地形非常熟悉，一直在追踪和提供其他错误信息。
你需要能够设身处地为你的对手着想，并像他们那样思考。盖尔策说，你需要正确的气质，“认真对待危险，而不是夸大它们(当你开始认真思考它们时，就会发生这种情况)。”同时也不要低估他们，“认知偏见和组织压力可以迫使我们这样做。
它们识别弱点或可能的攻击点，也就是斯塔莫斯所说的“攻击面”。对于每个看似合理的威胁，他们试图将对其后果程度的评估与发生的可能性分开，然后仔细地将这两种评估结合起来，以确定其总体紧迫性。正如盖尔策所说，他们研究如何防止攻击，如果攻击真的发生，如何减轻损害，以及如果攻击成功，需要采取什么样的应对措施。
威胁建模还会进入练习，帮助组织做好应对威胁的准备并更好地理解威胁。在Facebook，斯塔莫斯帮助组织了“红队”演习。他说：“红色团队是一支团队，要么是公司内部的，要么是从外部顾问那里雇来的，他们假装是对手，尽可能忠实地表现出他们的行为。”“在Facebook，我们的红色团队每年对公司进行两次大型演习。这些都是在研究一个真正的对手--比如说中华人民共和国国家安全部--的基础上制定的。“。
好的威胁建模的产品或交付内容是什么？它对您有什么帮助？
一个答案是：它可以帮助您部署稀缺资源。就像史塔莫斯对我说的，他的安保团队只有这么多人。他们只能承担这么多项目。威胁建模可以告诉您如何花费预算。与新闻编辑部的相似之处很明显：你们只有这么多记者。在选举前你能做的调查是有限的。特朗普上台后，新闻总是铺天盖地。你如何决定什么是紧急的？
另一个答案是：做得好，威胁建模--也就是所谓的威胁思维--让你的员工对他们的例行公事或假设可能掩盖的危险更加敏感。它是一种认知工具。
在前一篇文章中，我描述了一种可能出现的已发布产品：威胁紧迫度指数。它将结合对每个威胁的后果、可能性和紧迫性的评估，总结(对选举和美国民主)最大的危险，并对其进行排名。
索引将有一个网址。它会在有新的信息时更新，有点像538的选举预测。你也可以以时事通讯的形式订阅该索引。例如，现在，试图挑起人们对邮寄投票的恐惧可能会在名单上名列前茅。或者是呼吁武装民兵在有争议的选举中“保护”计票结果-这一呼吁呼应了特朗普的Twitter账户。
另一个用途可能是进行演习，提高新闻编辑部对新闻系统可能被操纵的认识，因为我们离选举越来越近。斯塔莫斯说：“最明显的一个就是被黑客窃取的文件。”“2016年工作得很好。为什么要换马呢？“。
正如史蒂夫·班农·特朗普(Steve Bannon Trump)中和新闻媒体的方法一样，他的方法是“用狗屎淹没这个地区”。总是有太多的事情需要注意。威胁建模可以通过将听起来可怕的事情与真正可怕的事情区分开来，从而帮助实现这一点。
这是一个答案。另一位来自“哥伦比亚新闻评论”的编辑凯尔·波普，
美国人民正生活在死亡和经济绝望的边缘。这些都是2020年选举的利害关系，多亏了选民压制，这场选举的完整性处于危险之中-以及(包括媒体必须超越过去的竞选报道，接受其作为
作为民主的保障，你不能只对从现在到1月20日的新闻中爆出的东西做出反应。你必须放大想象力，瞥见危险，然后努力回到今天和明天做出的决定。威胁建模可帮助您及时移动。
如果威胁建模是防御性的，那么记者应该试图捍卫的是什么？
对我来说，这是做这件事的一个原因。为了部署威胁建模或威胁“构思”团队，您必须知道您要防范的是什么。你必须承担起这个责任。这和报道接下来发生的事情有很大不同。
在竞选的早些时候，我写了一篇关于这个问题的帖子：如果不能自己牢牢把握住，你就不能不被卷入特朗普的议程。但是这个议程应该是什么呢？我认为，这必须是对美国民主及其核心仪式的某种捍卫：自由公正的选举，让人们对选举结果产生信任，从而使权力和平移交成为可能。
在现代早期，采访竞选活动的记者能够假设存在一个稳定的制度，因此专注于竞选本身。这在2020年是行不通的。因为不能保证我们会有自由和公平的投票。记者必须在合法选举的神圣土地上插上他们的旗帜，并帮助保卫它免受所有威胁。威胁建模可以帮助该项目。这就是我支持它被大型国家新闻提供商采用的理由。
唐纳德·特朗普不断散布有关冠状病毒的谎言，并积极试图破坏公众对选举结果的信心。当美国的民主岌岌可危时，媒体需要一种新的新闻报道方式。请在此处阅读我的文章
note_en:
As voters, as journalists, as citizens, and as writers, as participants alive in what was once considered a secure democracy, we are today living in the US through what Masha Gessen (following Bálint Magyar)
 Let those words sink in for a moment: an autocratic attempt … ”The build up to actually wielding autocratic power.”
 When a political figure who has gained power tries to use that power to probe the possibilities for establishing an autocratic state, that’s an autocratic attempt. The other stages in the process are an “autocratic breakthrough” and an “autocratic consolidation”. I hope we never get to either point. But I cannot say with confidence that we will not. Neither can Masha Gessen. (Read 		   Surviving Autocracy.)
 I recommended that the big national newsrooms create threat modelling teams to help organise coverage of the Trump government and the 2020 elections. My concern was that US democracy was being put at risk. Traditional campaign coverage is not capable of addressing that kind of threat.
  I consulted two people who have worked with threat modeling in other danger zones, and I asked them to help me imagine its possible uses in a newsroom setting.
 One is   currently a visiting professor of law at Georgetown University. From 2015 to 2017 he was senior director for counterterrorism on the National Security Council staff, where he participated in what are called “table top” exercises that try to imagine how threats would play out. (This is often called “threat ideation.”) As a “customer” working on counter-terrorism for the executive branch, he used the products of threat modeling teams based in the intelligence agencies and law enforcement. 		  about Donald Trump’s attacks on the 2020 election.
 My other informant is   former chief security officer at Facebook, and chief information security officer at Yahoo, which he described as “the most senior person at a company who is solely tasked with defending the company’s systems, software and other technical   Incorporating what I learned from Geltzer and Stamos – and from an off-the-record briefing about election threats put on by the Aspen Institute, which I attended recently – here is how I see it working.
 The recommendation: the big national news providers – ABC, CBS, NBC, CNN, NPR, PBS, AP, Reuters,  The  New York Times,  The  Washington Post – should have threat modelling teams, just as they all have pollsters. These teams would try to identify the most serious threats to a free and fair election and to US democracy   so that their newsrooms can take appropriate action.
  “Threat modelling is the effort to articulate dangers in ways that allow us to prepare to prevent, mitigate, or respond to them,” said Geltzer. “It’s a way of identifying and describing threats that helps us to address them.” Its purpose is to assess vulnerabilities and anticipate attacks in a more systematic fashion than just being worried about them.
 Stamos put it this way. “Threat modelling” is a formal process by which a team maps out the potential adversaries to a system and the capabilities of those adversaries, maps the attack surfaces of the system and the potential vulnerabilities in those attack surfaces, and then matches those two sets together to build a model of likely vulnerabilities and attacks.
  Geltzer told me: “You need to have a deep sense of what you’re trying to protect in the first place.” (I will come back to this point later; it is critical.) You also need expertise in the kinds of dangers that are likely to arise. For Trump as a threat to US democracy, 		  who have devoted serious study to “how democracies die”.
 For other kinds of threats newsrooms often have in-house expertise, in the form of beat reporters who know their terrain intimately, like NBC’s Brandy Zadrozny and Ben Collins, who have been tracking   and other misinformation rabbit holes.
 You need to be able to step into the shoes of your adversary, and think like they do. You need the right temperament, says Geltzer, “to take seriously dangers without inflating them (which can happen when you start thinking hard about them!) and also without underestimating them,” which cognitive bias and organisational pressures can make us do.
  They identify weaknesses or likely points of attack, what Stamos called “attack surfaces”. For each plausible threat they try to divide the assessment of how consequential it would be from how likely it is to happen, and then carefully combine those two to determine its overall urgency. As Geltzer said, they study how attacks can be prevented, how the damage can be mitigated if they do happen, and what kind of response is required if an attack succeeds.
 Threat modelling also flows into exercises that help an organisation prepare for threats and understand them better. At Facebook, Stamos helped run “red team” exercises. “A red team is a team, either internal to the company or hired from external consultants, that pretends to be an adversary and acts out their behaviour with as much fidelity as is possible,” he said. “At Facebook, our red team ran large exercises against the company twice a year. These would be composed based upon studying a real adversary – say, the Ministry of State Security of the People’s Republic of China.”
 What is the product – or deliverable – of good threat modelling, and what does it help you do?
 One answer: it helps you deploy scarce resources. As Stamos said to me, his security team had only so many people. They could only take on so many projects. Threat modeling can tell you how to spend your budget. The parallel to the newsroom is clear: you only have so many reporters. There is a limited number of investigations you can do before the election. With Trump in power there is always a flood of news. How do you decide what’s urgent?
 Another answer: done well, threat modelling – and what’s called threat ideation – makes your staff more alive to dangers that their routines or assumptions might have obscured. It’s an awareness tool.
  In a previous article, I described a published product that could emerge: A Threat Urgency Index. It would summarise and rank the biggest dangers (to the election and to US democracy) by combining assessments of how consequential, how likely, and how immediate each threat is.
 The index would have a web address. It would be updated when there is new information, sort of like Five Thirty Eight’s Election Forecast. You could also subscribe to the index as a newsletter. Right now, for example, the the attempt to whip up fears about mail-in voting might rank highly on that list. Or the call – echoing from Trump’s Twitter feed – for armed militias to “protect” the vote count in a disputed election.
 Another use might be to run exercises that raise newsroom awareness around the possible manipulation of the news system as we get closer to the election. “The obvious one is hacked documents,” said Stamos. “Worked great in 2016. Why change horses?”
  As Steve Bannon 		  Trump’s method for neutralising the news media is to “flood the zone with shit”. There’s always too many things to pay attention to. Threat modelling could help with that by separating things that sound scary from things that really are scary – and could happen.
 That’s one answer. Another comes from Kyle Pope, the editor of  Columbia Journalism Review,
 "The American people are living on the edge of death and economic despair. Those are the stakes of the 2020 election, one whose integrity is in jeopardy thanks to 		  and 		  on top of voter suppression – 		  and 		  (including 		  The press must look past the campaign coverage that was and embrace its role as
 To be a safeguard of democracy you cannot just react to what explodes into the news from now until 20 January. You have to zoom forward in imagination, glimpse danger, and then work your way back to decisions made today and tomorrow. Threat modelling helps you move about in time.
 If threat modelling is defensive, what is it that journalists should be trying to defend?
 To me this is one reason to do it. In order to deploy a threat modelling, or threat “ideation” team you have to know what you are trying to protect against. You have to own that responsibility. Which is a lot different from reporting whatever comes down the pike.
 Earlier in the campaign, I wrote a post about this problem:  You cannot keep from getting swept up in Trump’s agenda without a firm grasp on your own. But what should that agenda be? I think it has to be some kind of defence of US democracy and its central ritual: free and fair elections that engender trust in the outcome, and thereby make the peaceful transfer of power possible.
 Earlier in the modern era, journalists covering election campaigns had been able to assume the existence of a stable system, and therefore focus on the contest itself. That doesn’t work for 2020. For it is by no means guaranteed that we will have a free and fair vote. Journalists have to plant their flag on the sacred ground of legitimate elections, and help defend it against all threats. Threat modeling can assist with that project. And that is my argument for its adoption by the big national news providers.
              Donald Trump consistently spreads lies about the coronavirus and actively tries to undermine public confidence in the results of the election. When US democracy is at stake, the media needs a new way of doing journalism.  					Read my article here
title: 为了保护民主，记者需要学会像黑客和间谍一样思考。
title_en: To protect democracy, journalists need to learn to think like hackers and spies

地址是错误假设的沃土，因为每个人都习惯于处理地址，而且99%的情况下它们看起来非常简单。以下是我见过的一些错误假设，或者我自己做过的，或者已经向我报告过的。(如果你想查找英国邮政编码的地址，反之亦然，以确认我正在告诉你的是什么，试试皇家邮政邮政编码查找器。)(如果你想查找英国邮政编码的地址，或者反之亦然，可以试试皇家邮政邮政编码查找器(Royal Mail Postcode Finder)。)。
我们可以将这些有趣的数字输入到建筑物名称字段中，因为没有建筑物既有名称又有有趣的数字。
50 Ammanford Road，Tycroes，Ammanford，SA18 3QJ和50 Ammanford Road，Llandybie，Ammanford，SA18 3YF之间的差异约为4英里(谷歌地图)。
您还可以获得套房编号、楼层编号、单元编号和名称中带有数字的组织。
Adrien Piérard贡献了一个来自日本的地址，它有15位数字，有6个单独的数字(如果将邮政编码算作一个数字，则有5个)。格式是：980-0804(邮政编码)，宫城县(县)仙台市(市)青瓦区(区)国邦町(区)4-10-20(街道号街区号地段号)仙台(楼名)401(公寓号)。
Benton Lam提供这个来自香港特别行政区的地址-它的道路上(14)和它的建筑群(3)上都有一个号码：香港特别行政区东太古湾道14号城市广场3号15楼。
Tibor Schütz指出，可以跳过建筑编号-例如，在一条街道上，一侧是偶数编号的建筑，另一侧是奇数编号的建筑；多个建筑共享相同的编号(例如，在已经建造新房子的地方)，以及具有多个编号的建筑。
西里尔·谢佩洛夫(Cyrille Chépélov)和萨米·莱蒂宁(Sami Lehtinen)告诉我，在法国昂蒂布和芬兰乡村，一些建筑是根据距离道路起点的距离进行编号的--比如朗罗德65号(LongROAD 65)代表距离朗罗德(LongROAD)起点750米的建筑。
如果道路左侧的地址是偶数，则右侧的地址一定是奇数。
西里尔·谢佩洛夫指出，在一些地方，法国塞纳圣丹尼的蒙特勒伊苏伊尔大道(Boulevard Théophile Sueur)两边都有平分。双方也在不同的城市和发展中。
本·蒂利报道美国马萨诸塞州波士顿02109邮局10号-据报道，这与美国马萨诸塞州波士顿02109邮局10号不同。
肖恩·克兰普顿(Shaun Crampton)报道说，他住在帕洛阿尔托阿尔马街101号001号公寓--1号和001号公寓位于不同的楼层。
道格拉斯·佩雷奥特(Douglas Perreault)报告说，他住在一个共管公寓协会内的一个街区里；这是一个很大的协会，从A区到Z区，然后是Alpha、Beta、Gamma、Delta和Theta。邮件和投递经常从阿尔法区块误发到A区块，反之亦然。他当时的地址是：佛罗里达州坦帕市阿尔法39号北纬46街14100号，邮编33613。
据Jan Jongom报道，荷兰的街道可以编号，例如奈梅亨的普莱恩1944年。
当有编号的街道和门牌号时，它们之间会有分隔符。
街道名称可以是完整的描述符：伦敦希尔大街17号，伦敦W1J 5LJ或安大略省多伦多大道路。
好的，但是如果有描述符，它会在街道名称的开头或结尾。
丹、弗雷德·克罗恩、大卫·安德伍德和丹尼尔·迪克森提交了分数街道编号的例子，比如宾夕法尼亚州匹兹堡43/5街，以及分数建筑编号。这些代码可以用Unicode(43 1/2 ST)、带斜杠的分数(43 1/2)或小数(43.5)编写。
朱利安·弗莱舍(Julian Fleischer)提供了一个来自德国Bocholt的例子，展示了几条非常接近的道路，都被称为de Welle。
柯克·克雷克斯花了几年的时间，使用了俄克拉何马州詹宁斯的2-N然后3-W形式的地址，该地址经常收到成功的送货服务，邮编为74038&34；。迈克·莱利(Mike Riley)曾在美国新墨西哥州索科罗以西50英里(80公里)的地方邮寄甚大阵列射电望远镜。
萨姆向我指着梅诺莫尼瀑布，那里的房屋是用密尔沃基县的网格系统而不是门牌号来寻址的--给出了N88 W16541 Foobar St.这样的地址。
安迪·莫纳特(Andy Monat)在杜兰大学(Tulane University)一个学期的海外项目中发了以下地址：哥斯达黎加圣何塞Curridabat的Hyperma/Walmart以北50米的Ciapa。Adrien Piérard和Luke Allardyce指出，街道名称在日本很少使用，取而代之的是使用地区、街区和地段编号(更多关于日语地址系统的维基百科条目的信息)。2002年的一篇“世界新闻评论”(World Press Review)的一篇报道给出了这样的示例地址：从曾经的中国餐厅出发，两个街区外，向湖边走半个街区，隔壁是那辆黄色汽车停放的房子，位于尼加拉瓜马那瓜。肖恩·克兰普顿(Shaun Crampton)发来了一篇文章，介绍了尼加拉瓜制度的更多细节和例子。斯蒂格·布劳塔塞(Stig Brautaset)指出，英国广播公司(BBC)一篇关于喀布尔邮报的文章给出了这个例子：哈米德·扬(Hamid Jaan)，达鲁尔-阿曼宫(Darul-Aman)后面。据内森·费尔曼报道，尼加拉瓜和哥斯达黎加也使用了类似的称谓。
Paul Puschmann和Tibor Schütz指出，德国的曼海姆市有时被称为Quadratestadt(广场之城)，因为市中心是以网格形式排列的，街区被分配一个字母(沿南北轴线)和一个数字(沿着东西轴线)，然后建筑物按街区编号编号。因此，R5区块6至13号的示例地址为：Institut für Deutsche Sprache，R5，6-13，D-68161 Mannheim。
莱昂尼·卢宾吉举了一个南非地址的例子：比勒陀利亚，Strydfontein 306 Jr，第84部分，意思是小Strydfontein 306农场的第84块地块。
很多道路，比如车道、上坡道和停车场的过道都没有名字。日本的一些道路也没有名字，因为流行的寻址系统适用于地区、分区、街区、地段和地段编号。
彼得·肯威指出，在美国，一些家庭被称为乡村路线，在一条覆盖多条道路的路线上，数字被分配给箱子。例如：Box1234，R.R.1，Winthrop，ME 04364。
从伦敦的戈斯韦尔路到爱丁堡的摄政道，许多不同的道路组成了410英里的A1。虽然可能只有一个Goswell路1号和只有一个摄政道1号，但在这条指定为A1的道路上有多个编号为1的建筑。
道路也可以用多种语言命名。例如，在爱尔兰，道路可能同时用英语和爱尔兰语命名。
皇家邮政有他们所谓的从属街道--例如：英国伯明翰伦康姆路榆树大道6号，邮编：B128QX(伦康康路是这条街，榆树大道是矮小的从属街道，在这座城市里不是独一无二的。谷歌地图)。
另一个反例：流氓头发，1霍普顿游行，斯特里森大街，伦敦，SW16 6EP(斯特里森大街就是这条街。霍普顿游行是路上的一小排商店--谷歌地图)。
皇家邮政将点名投递到某些地点，如农场和小屋。例如：奥克兰，费尔西特，七橡树，TN15 7LT，英国(费尔西特是城镇-这实际上是在维戈路上。谷歌地图)。
大型收件人有时仅按组织收件人-例如：选举改革协会有限公司，伦敦，N1 1RS，英国
伊恩·帕里斯(Iain Parris)和纳特(Naath)都指出，虽然许多网站要求用户提供一个县，但并不是所有的国家都使用它们来称呼。例如，皇家邮政在1996年停止使用邮政县。
Yves Daoust报告说，在比利时，地址只需要街道、邮政编码和城市；例如：欧尔班大道(Boulevard Frère Orban)，27,4000列日。Erik Engheim报告说，在挪威，奥斯陆是一个由(市)、Tettsted(市区)、Komome(市)和Fylke(县)组成的城市，但它通常在书面地址中只出现一次。
Jzwinck指出，新加坡是一个城市国家，如果你需要城市、县和国家，就会有新加坡、新加坡-甚至新加坡、新加坡、新加坡这样的地址。
Jan Jongom从荷兰报道，那里有两个城市叫Eursinge-在同一个省！
理查德·弗莱彻、李·霍斯蒂、保罗·O·诺兰、西蒙·戴亚布、托尼·芬恩、唐纳尔·麦卡锡、马克·林奇和多诺万都指出，爱尔兰共和国等国家的地址没有邮政编码，或者只在该国某些地区有邮政编码。丹尼·安格斯(Danny Angus)指出，香港的情况也是如此。
杰西卡·恩德斯指出AEC的邮寄地址：澳大利亚选举委员会，回复支付9867，您的首都城市。
当然，大多数用户都会看到，但我在公司信头上看到过错误的邮政编码！误读手写邮政编码似乎很常见。
虽然美国的邮政编码通常覆盖很大的区域，但安东尼·伊利桑多指出，一些建筑也有自己的邮政编码--比如帝国大厦(10118)
格雷厄姆·李(Graham Lee)指出，DVLA斯旺西(这就是整个地址)用邮政编码来标识不同的部门：V5C在SA991BA处理，驾照在SA991AB处理，依此类推。
但恩菲尔德议会网站的联系页面上列出了他们的邮政编码为EN1 3XY-皇家邮政认为这是分拣局的一个邮政信箱。
在英国，这大多是合理的，但也有某些例外。例如，CV4 7AL是华威大学的邮政编码，一个邮政编码代表住在校园里的6000名学生和在校园工作的学者/行政人员。
此外，如果你的客户在度假时收到了送货上门的东西，许多客户的账户上可能会有相同的度假公园。
一些地址对应于灵活的办公空间，以及提供听起来像豪华办公室的邮政信箱的组织。皇家邮政列出了90多家在伦敦老布罗德街25号42号塔楼运营的组织，EC2N 1HQ。度假公园和小屋也可能出现在许多顾客的账户上。
Victor Nicollet提供了法国邮政编码75015的例子，它代表了覆盖230,000多人的巴黎XV区。
据迈克·科恩报道，邮政编码33334覆盖了3个城市：奥克兰公园、威尔顿庄园和劳德代尔堡，这三个城市都在佛罗里达州。
一些巴西的邮政编码是按照Speeder的，以色列军队的邮政编码也是如此。杰西卡·恩德斯(Jessica Enders)和弗兰克·马尔科姆(Frank Malcolm)指出，澳大利亚北领地的邮政编码以08开头；例如，达尔文市的邮政编码是0800。Morsillo Lindsay，Thomas Norris和Jerry B.Altzman指出，美国东北部的地址邮政编码以零开始，例如美国马萨诸塞州波士顿02109，10号邮局；有些邮政编码以两个零开始。据Mikael Vejdemo-Johansson报道，德国耶拿的邮政编码是07737。
安蒂·维克曼(Antti Vikman)和约翰·迈伦(Johan Myréen)告诉我，芬兰首都赫尔辛基的所有邮政编码都以两个零开头。一些特殊代码甚至以四个零(00002赫尔辛基)开头。据安德鲁·琼斯报道，法国邮政编码的首位是départements*，可能会使用前导零。寄往格拉斯06130的邮件被正确投递到阿尔卑斯马里泰斯06区的小镇-但寄往6130格拉斯的邮件首先被送往奥恩61部门。据克莱兹报道，意大利邮政代号(CAP)可以有一个前导零。
除了科西嘉-西里尔·谢佩洛夫报告说，它被分成两部分：2A和2B，但邮局保留了原来的20号(阿雅克肖，20000；巴斯蒂亚，20200)。
如果地址中可以包含组织和部门名称，则不会！例如：环境、食品和农村事务部(D E F R A)，国家兽医局，动物卫生办公室，Hadrian House，Wavell Drive，Rosehill Industrial Estate，Carlisle，CA12，2TB，英国。
另一个例子：英国查特豪斯广场皇家医学院妇科圣巴塞洛美斯妇科妇科癌症研究组，邮编：EC1M 6GR。
格雷厄姆·莱因德建议波斯尼亚比哈奇使用这个89个字符的街道名称：Aleja Alije Izetbegoića Prvig Predsjednika PredsjednišTva ReRepublic ika Bosna i Herceenda。
您需要8条线路和8个国家/地区才能送货到：GB技术服务部，单位W7a，Warwick House，18 Forge Lane，Minworth，Minworth，Sutton Coldfield，B76 1AH，英国。
地址不包含逗号(这样我就可以用逗号替换换行符，然后用换行符逗号，然后回到原来的位置)。
地址可以包含组织名称，组织名称可以包含逗号。例如：学院协会，国家和大学图书馆，102 Euston Street，London，NW12公顷
伦敦金斯伯里路10号，伦敦，N1 4AZ，圣犹大和圣保罗圣保罗E(弗吉尼亚州)小学。
1 Highview Terrace，Westward Ho！，Bideford，EX39 1AQ(某些数据库中省略了感叹号)。
马克·华莱士(Mark Wallace)告诉我们，荷兰的海牙有多个正式名称，其中之一是Gravenhage(不是复数，字面意思是以撇号s开头并带有连字符的城市名称)。
更广泛地说，地址可以包含组织名称，组织可以使用像Yahoo！这样的名称。带着感叹号。关于公司的立法规定了英国允许使用的字符，并允许公司名称包括！$LTD(公司08209948)；@LTD(公司08209882)；$LTD(公司08209885)和%LTD(公司04487680)以及各种我没有的其他名称，因为这些公司的网站不会让我搜索它们。
西蒙·韦斯科特指出，数据库只会定期发布，所以新建筑被省略并不少见。
皇家邮政和地形测量局的数据可以包含已拆除的建筑和正在建设的建筑。皇家邮政甚至有一种名为“尚未建立”的数据库产品。临时邮政编码甚至可以分配给建筑工地！
克里斯托弗·艾伦(Christopher Allen)指出，住在新公寓楼和船厂船屋的人有时需要在官方地址前加上船名或公寓号码。
XaspR8d一定对他的路被称为S Hwy X&34；、高速公路X&34；和南县路X&34；这一事实感到恼火，并讲述了一个无法购买产品的故事，因为唯一通过供应商地址验证的地址将无法通过他的银行的地址验证，反之亦然。
乔恩·彼得森(Jon Peterson)住在一个公寓区，这个公寓区分为鹌鹑岭环路、鹌鹑岭东巷和鹌鹑岭西巷。似乎只有美国邮政总局和城市电力公司承认这条小巷。其他所有人都要求缩短为鹌鹑Rdg E&34；(或W)，据报道，当UPS将包裹交给USPS时，包裹被退回为无法投递-没有这样的地址。
苏珊娜·弗莱明(Susannah Fleming)住在皇家邮政称之为Top O&#39；the Lane，Brindle，Chorley，PR68PA的路上。她报告说，不同数据库中的表示包括：
一个离我只有几个座位的开发商最近带来了一艘船屋，打算把它作为她的主要住所。不用说，船可以移动-包括在城镇之间，甚至在国家之间！
弗洛斯蒂指出了伊利诺伊州对地址的包罗万象的方法：如果你没有街道地址，请在下面描述你的家：列出分区的名称；交叉街道；道路；地标；里程和/或邻居的名字。
莎伦·弗拉斯(Sharon Freas)处理过支持雪鸟(Snowbird)客户的系统，这些客户会随着季节的变化而在不同的地址之间轮换。
地址可以在县和行政区之间移动。例如，我曾经住在格温特县，但现在已经不存在了。还可以为地址分配新的邮政编码。
苏珊·施密特指出，东欧的城市、街道和整个国家都被重新命名-例如，列宁作为街道名称不再受欢迎。地址可以在有争议的领土上，甚至可以在战区。
道格拉斯·佩雷奥特报告说，自己拥有的一套共管公寓地址更改了三次；首先是佛罗里达州阿尔法39号北46街14100号，FL 33549；然后新建了一座邮局，成为佛罗里达州阿尔法39号坦帕市北46街14100号，FL 33612；然后邮政编码改为14100 N 46街，阿尔法39号，坦帕市，FL 33613；然后共管公寓协会改为不太容易出错的街区命名方案，给出了14410号悬挂苔藓圈，101号，坦帕市，FL 33613。
正如你可以从维基百科关于邮政县的页面上了解到的那样，即使当皇家邮政使用邮政县时，他们也并不总是与行政县站在一起。当然，随着地方政府结构的变化，行政区来来去去-例如，我出生的那个县已经不复存在了。
彼得·贝利(Peter Bailey)指出，有几个国家有特殊的军事邮件，用于处理向部署在其他国家的士兵、海上船只和类似人员递送邮件的复杂性；这些国家的地址并不总是遵循传统的地址格式。例如，地址BFP0，BF1 4FB是海军舰艇HMS的地址。
据埃德·希贝尔报道，分配给以色列军队部队的邮政编码与这些部队一起四处游荡。
像邮政信箱这样的地址通常只能和收件人所在的城市或分拣办公室一样精确。Jessica Enders告诉我澳大利亚邮政服务回复付费地址(不需要邮票)；邮政信箱(邮政信箱)；GPO信箱(仅限首府城市中心的邮政总局信箱)；带锁的袋子；私人袋子；包裹储物柜；包裹收件；#34；邮局转交；CMA(社区邮件代理)；CPA(社区邮政代理)；CMB(社区邮袋)和邮件服务(MS)！
Tibor Schütz指出，许多邮局处理给圣诞老人的邮件都很新颖，甚至还分配了特殊的邮政编码。例如，在德国：圣诞老人Nordpolen，Julemandes Postkontor，DK-3900Nuuk；在加拿大：圣诞老人，北极，H0H0H0；在英国：圣诞老人，圣诞老人洞穴，驯鹿地，XM4 5HQ。
Matthieu Valleton联系到了他在印度洋的法国领土Kerguelen岛(Google地图)上的地址，他的地址是Kerguelen区(岛)，Terres australes et antarctique Françaises(领土)，通过留尼旺岛(指明邮件应该经过的地方)，法国(国家)。
蒙蒂指出，不寻常的政治等级制度可能会导致不寻常的邮寄地址。例如，整个福克兰群岛共享邮政编码Fiqq1ZZ。另一方面，英属维尔京群岛有自己的邮政编码系统。
大卫·库德给我看了1990年的一篇文章：“风险文摘”的记者蒂姆·凯在把邮件寄到他的大学校园鸽子洞时遇到了问题：蒂莫西·L·凯，信箱256-80，加利福尼亚州帕萨迪纳市，邮编：91125。据报道，自动系统将他的邮政编码改为91102。大卫·库德认为这是因为帕萨迪纳所有的邮政信箱都在91102号信箱里。
阿拉斯泰尔·霍顿提醒我们，希腊税务局的地址是Χανδρή1&amp；Θεσσαλονίκης，Τ.Κ。18346，Αθήνα。
维基百科有一张包裹的照片，上面有一个俄语/西里尔文地址显示在电脑上，字符编码错误，并从那里转录出来。据报道，一名俄罗斯邮政工作人员颠倒了地图，并递送了包裹。

阿拉斯泰尔·霍顿(Alastair Houghton)指出，地址可能会用来源国的字符集写成。
国际邮件可以用源国家和目的地国家的字符集指定国家(以及可能的其他详细信息)，因此两端的邮政工作人员都可以阅读。
阿拉斯泰尔·霍顿提供了这个日本地址的例子：〒1008994(邮政编码)，東京都(东京都，即东京县或州)中央区(中央区，即中央区)八重洲一丁目(八州1区，即四州第一小区)5番3号(第5地块3)，東京中央郵便局(东京中央邮政局)。感谢诺曼·戴蒙德告诉我地址的哪些部分是哪些！

note_en:
Addressing is a fertile ground for incorrect assumptions, because everyone's used to dealing with addresses and 99% of the time they seem so simple.Below are some incorrect assumptions I've seen made, or made myself, or had reported to me.(If you want to look up an address for a UK postcode or vice-versa to confirm what I'm telling you, try the  Royal Mail Postcode Finder)
 We can put those funny numbers into the building name field, as no buildings have both a name and a funny number
  The difference between 50 Ammanford Road, Tycroes, Ammanford, SA18 3QJ and 50 Ammanford Road, Llandybie, Ammanford, SA18 3YF is about 4 miles ( Google Maps).
 You also get suite numbers, floor numbers, unit numbers, and organisations with numbers in their names.
 Adrien Piérard contributes an address from Japan with fifteen digits in six separate numbers (five if you count the zip code as a single number). The format is: 980-0804 (zip code), Miyagi-ken (prefecture) Sendai-shi (city) Aoba-ku (ward) Kokubuncho (district) 4-10-20 (sub-district-number block-number lot-number) Sendai (building name) 401 (flat number).
  Benton Lam offers this address from the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region - it has both a number on its road (14) and in its group of buildings (3): 15/F, Cityplaza 3, 14 TaiKoo Wan Road, Island East, HKSAR
 Tibor Schütz points out building numbers may be skipped - for example, on a street where even-numbered buildings are on one side, odd numbers on the other; multiple buildings sharing the same number (such as where a new house has been built) and buildings with more than one number.
 Cyrille Chépélov and Sami Lehtinen tell me in Antibes, France and rural Finland some buildings are numbered based on the distance from the start of the road - such as Longroad 65 for the building 750m from the start of longroad.
 If the addresses on the left of the road are even, the addresses on the right must be odd
 Cyrille Chépélov points out that in places,  Boulevard Théophile Sueur, Montreuil, Seine-Saint-Denis, France has evens-only on both sides. The two sides are also in different cities and Départements.
 Ben Tilly reports on Ten Post Office Sq, Boston MA 02109 USA - which is not, reportedly, the same as 10 Post Office Sq, Boston MA 02109 USA.
 Shaun Crampton reports living at 101 Alma St, Apartment 001, Palo Alto - where apartments 1 and 001 were on different floors.
 Douglas Perreault reports he lived in a block within a condo association; it was a large association, with blocks A through Z then Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, and Theta. Mail and deliveries were often misrouted from block Alpha to block A and vice-versa. His address at the time was: 14100 N 46th St., Alpha 39, Tampa, FL 33613
 Jan Jongboom reports streets can be numbered in the Netherlands - for example, Plein 1944 in Nijmegen.
 When there's a numbered street and a house number, there will be a separator between them
  A street name can be entirely descriptors: 17 Hill Street, London, W1J 5LJ or  Avenue Road, Toronto, Ontario.
  OK, but if there's a descriptor it'll be at the start or end of the street name.
  Dan, Fred Kroon, David Underwood and Daniel Dickison submitted examples of fractional street numbers like  43rd ½ St, Pittsburgh, PA, and of fractional building numbers. These can be written in unicode (43rd ½ St), as a fraction with a slash (43 1/2) or as a decimal (43.5)
  Julian Fleischer provides an example from Bocholt in Germany showing several roads in close proximity all called  Up de Welle.
 Kirk Kerekes spent several years using an address of the form "2 mi N then 3 mi W of Jennings, OK 74038" which regularly got successful deliveries. Mike Riley used to mail the Very Large Array radio telescope at "50 miles (80 km) West of Socorro, New Mexico, USA"
 Sam pointed me to  Menomonee Falls where houses are addressed using Milwaukee County's grid system instead of house numbers - giving addresses like N88 W16541 Foobar St.
 Andy Monat sent the following address example, from a  semester abroad program at Tulane University : CIAPA, 50 meters north of the Hypermas/Walmart of Curridabat, San Jose, Costa Rica. Adrien Piérard and Luke Allardyce point out street names are seldom used in Japan - instead, districts and blocks and lot numbers are used (more info on the  Wikipedia entry for the Japanese addressing system). A  2002 World Press Review report gave this sample address: From where the Chinese restaurant used to be, two blocks down, half a block toward the lake, next door to the house where the yellow car is parked, Managua, Nicaragua. Shaun Crampton sent  an article with more details and examples of the Nicaraguan system. Stig Brautaset pointed out  a BBC article about post in Kabul gives this example: "Hamid Jaan, behind Darul-Aman palace". Nathan Fellman reports similar addressing is used in Nicaragua and Costa Rica.
 Paul Puschmann and Tibor Schütz pointed out the city of  Mannheim in Germany is sometimes called Quadratestadt (City of Squares) as the city centre is arranged in a grid, with blocks assigned a letter (along the north-south axis) and a number (along the east-west axis) then buildings numbered by block number. So an example address at numbers 6 to 13 on block R 5 would be: Institut für Deutsche Sprache, R 5, 6-13, D-68161 Mannheim
 Leoni Lubbinge gives an example of a South African address: Part 84, Strydfontein 306 JR, Pretoria which means the 84th plot of the farm Strydfontein 306 JR.
 Plenty of roads like driveways, onramps and the aisles of carparks don't have names. Some roads in Japan also don't have names, as  the prevalent addressing system works on districts, subdistricts, blocks, lots and lot numbers.
 Peter Kenway points out in America some homes are addressed as Rural Routes, where numbers are allocated to boxes on a route covering multiple roads. For example: Box 1234, R.R. 1, Winthrop, ME 04364.
 Many different roads, from Goswell Road in London to Regent Road in Edinburgh, make up the 410 mile  A1. And while there may only be one "1 Goswell Road" and only one "1 Regent Road" there are multiple buildings numbered 1 on the road designated A1.
 Roads may also be named in multiple languages. For example, in Ireland roads may be named in both English and Irish
 The Royal Mail have what they call a 'dependent street' - for example: 6 Elm Avenue, Runcorn Road, Birmingham, B12 8QX, United Kingdom (Runcorn Road is the street, Elm Avenue is the stubby 'dependent street' and isn't unique within the city.  Google Maps )
 Another counterexample: Rogue Hair, 1 Hopton Parade, Streatham High Road, London, SW16 6EP (Streatham High Road is the street. Hopton Parade is a little row of shops on the road -  Google Maps )
 The Royal Mail will deliver to certain premises by name, such as farms and cottages. Example: Oakland, Fairseat, Sevenoaks, TN15 7LT, United Kingdom (Fairseat is the town - this is actually on Vigo Road.  Google Maps )
  Large recipients of mail are sometimes addressed by organisation alone - for example: Electoral Reform Society Ltd, London, N1 1RS, United Kingdom
 Iain Parris and Naath both pointed out that, while many websites ask users for a county, not all countries use them in addressing. For example,  the Royal Mail stopped using postal counties in 1996.
 Yves Daoust reports that in Belgium an address only requires a street, postcode and city; example: Boulevard Frère Orban, 27, 4000 Liège. Erik Engheim reports that in Norway Oslo is a By (city), Tettsted (urban area), Kommune (municipality) and Fylke (county) but it usually only appears once in a written address.
 jzwinck points out Singapore is a city-state, leading to addresses like Singapore, Singapore - or even Singapore, Singapore, Singapore if you demand a city, county and country.
 Jan Jongboom reports from the Netherlands, where there are two cities called Eursinge - in the same province!
  Richard Fletcher, Lee Hosty, Paul O'Nolan, Simon Diab, Tony Finn, Donal Maccarthy, mark lynch and Donovan all pointed out countries like the Republic of Ireland have addresses without postcodes, or only have postcodes in certain parts of the country. Danny Angus points out this is also the case in Hong Kong.
 Jessica Enders points out  the post-paid address of the AEC: Australian Electoral Commission, Reply Paid 9867, your capital city
 Most users will, of course, but I've seen incorrect postcodes on corporate letterheads! Misreading handwritten postcodes seems like a common one.
 Although zip codes in the US usually cover very large areas, Anthony Elizondo points out some buildings have their own zip codes - like the Empire State Building (10118)
 Graham Lee points out DVLA Swansea (that's the whole address), where different departments are identified by postcode: V5Cs are processed at SA99 1BA, driving licences at SA99 1AB and so on.
  But the Enfield council website contact page lists their postcode as EN1 3XY - which the Royal Mail think is a  PO Box at the sorting office.
 Mostly this is reasonable in the UK, but there are certain exceptions. For example CV4 7AL is the postcode of the University of Warwick - one postcode for 6,000 students living on campus, and academics/administrators working on campus.
 Also, if your customers get things delivered to them when they're on holiday, lots of customers may have the same holiday parks on their accounts.
 Some addresses correspond to 'flexible office spaces' and organisations that offer PO Boxes that sound like fancy offices. The Royal Mail lists more than 90 organisations operating out of Tower 42, 25 Old Broad Street, London, EC2N 1HQ. Holiday parks and cottages may also appear on many customers' accounts.
 Victor Nicollet contributes the example of French postcode 75015, representing the XVth arrondissement of Paris, which covers over 230,000 people.
 Mike Cohen reports zip code 33334 covers 3 cities: Oakland Park, Wilton Manors, and Fort Lauderdale, all in Florida.
 Some Brazilian zip codes do according to speeder, as do  Israeli postcodes for army units. Jessica Enders and Frank Malcolm pointed out postcodes in the Northern Territory of Australia start with 08; for example the  postcode for the city Darwin is 0800. Morsillo Lindsay, Thomas Norris and Jerry B. Altzman point out American addresses in the north east have zip codes starting with zero, for example Ten Post Office Sq, Boston MA 02109, USA; and some zip codes start with two zeros. Mikael Vejdemo-Johansson reports the zip code of Jena in Germany is 07737.
 Antti Vikman and Johan Myréen tell me all postcodes in Helsinki, the capital of Finland, all start with two zeroes. Some special codes even start with four zeroes (00002 HELSINKI). Andrew Jones reports the initial digits of French postcodes are the départements*, and may use a leading zero. Post addressed to 06130 Grasse is correctly delivered to the town in district 06, Alpes Maritimes - but post addressed to 6130 Grasse is first routed to department 61, Orne. klez reports Italian Codice di Avviamento Postale (CAP) numbers can have a leading zero.
 Except Corsica - Cyrille Chépélov reports it's split into départements 2A and 2B, but the Post Office kept the former single-number 20 (Ajaccio, 20000; Bastia, 20200)
 Not when organisation and department names can be included in addresses! For example: Department For Environment Food & Rural Affairs (D E F R A), State Veterinary Service, Animal Health Office, Hadrian House, Wavell Drive, Rosehill Industrial Estate, Carlisle, CA1 2TB, United Kingdom
 Another example: The Gynaecology Cancer Research Unit, Department of Obstetrics & Gynaecology, St. Bartholomews & The Royal School of Medicine & Dentistry, Charterhouse Square, London, EC1M 6GR, United Kingdom
 Graham Rhind suggests this 89-character street name in Bihac, Bosnia: Aleja Alije Izetbegovića Prvig Predsjednika Predsjedništva Republika Bosna i Hercegovina
 You'll need 8 lines and country to deliver to: GB Technical Services, Unit W7a, Warwick House, 18 Forge Lane, Minworth Industrial Park, Minworth, Sutton Coldfield, B76 1AH, United Kingdom
 Addresses don't contain commas (so I can replace newlines with commas then commas with newlines and get back to where I was)
 Addresses can contain organisation names, and organisation names can contain commas. For example: Society of College, National & University Libraries, 102 Euston Street, London, NW1 2HA
 St. Judes & St. Pauls C of E (Va) Primary School, 10 Kingsbury Road, London, N1 4AZ
  1 Highview Terrace,  Westward Ho!, Bideford, EX39 1AQ (exclamation mark is omitted in some databases)
   Mark Wallace tells us The Hague in the Netherlands is has multiple official names, one of which is  's-Gravenhage (not a plural, literally a city name starting apostrophe s and with a hyphen)
  More generally, addresses can contain organisation names, and organisations can have names like Yahoo! with an exclamation mark. Legislation on company  sets out the allowable characters for the UK and it permits company names including ! LTD (company 08209948); @ LTD (company 08209882); $ LTD (company 08209885) and % LTD (company 04487680) as well as a variety of other names I don't have examples for as the companies house website won't let me search for them.
 Simon Westcott points out databases are only released periodically, so it's not unusual for new buildings to be omitted.
 Royal Mail and Ordnance Survey data can contain demolished buildings, and buildings currently under construction. The Royal Mail even have a database product called  Not Yet Built. Temporary postcodes can even be assigned to building sites!
 Christopher Allen points out that people in new blocks of flats and houseboats in boatyards sometimes need to prefix their official address with a boat name or flat number.
 XaspR8d must have been exasperated by the fact his road is variously known as "S Hwy X", "Highway X" and "South County Rd X" - and related a story of being unable to buy a product as the only addresses that passed a vendor's address validation wouldn't pass his bank's address validation and vice-versa.
 Jon Peterson lives in an apartment community that is divided into Quail Ridge Cir, Quail Ridge East Lane and Quail Ridge West Lane. Only the USPS and the city electric utility seem to recognize the "Lane". Everyone else requires it be shortened to "Quail Rdg E" (or W) and reportedly when UPS turns a package over to the USPS it gets returned as undeliverable-no such address.
 Susannah Fleming lived on the road the Royal Mail call Top O'The Lane, Brindle, Chorley, PR6 8PA. She reports representations in different databases include:
 A developer just a few seats from me recently brought a  house boat with the intention of using it as her primary residence. Needless to say, boats can move - including between towns and even countries!
 fr0sty points out the  State of Illinois catch-all approach to addresses: "IF YOU HAVE NO STREET ADDRESS, below describe your home: list the name of subdivision; cross streets; roads; landmarks; mileage and/or neighbors' names."
 Sharon Freas has dealt with systems supporting " Snowbird" clients, who alternate between addresses with the changing seasons.
 Addresses can move between counties and administrative districts. For example, I used to live in  the county Gwent, but that no longer exists. Addresses may also be assigned new postcodes.
 Susanne Schmidt points out cities, streets and entire countries were renamed in eastern europe - for example, when Lenin fell out of favour as a street name. Addresses can be in disputed territories, or even war zones.
 Douglas Perreault reports owning a condo that changed address three times; first it was 14100 N 46th St., Alpha 39, Lutz, FL 33549; then a new post office was built and it became 14100 N 46th St., Alpha 39, Tampa, FL 33612; then the ZIP Code changed giving 14100 N 46th St., Alpha 39, Tampa, FL 33613; then the condo association changed to a less error-prone block naming scheme, giving 14410 Hanging Moss Circle, #101, Tampa, FL 33613.
 As you can learn from  wikipedia's page on postal counties, even when the Royal Mail used postal counties, they didn't always line up with administrative counties. And of course administrative regions come and go with changes to local government structures - for example,  the county I was born in in no longer exists.
 Peter Bailey points out several countries have special  military mail to deal with the complexities of delivering to soldiers deployed to other countries, ships at sea and similar; and their addresses don't always follow conventional address formats. For example, the address BFPO, BF1 4FB is the address of the navy vessel  HMS Example.
 Ed Schiebel reports the postcodes allocated to Israeli army units roam around with the units.
 Addresses such as  PO boxes are often only as precise as the recipient's city or sorting office. Jessica Enders tells me the Australian post service Reply Paid addresses (no stamp needed); PO Boxes (Post Office Boxes); GPO Boxes (General Post Office Boxes, in the middle of capital cities only); locked bags; private bags; parcel lockers; parcel collect; "Care of Post Office"; CMA (Community Mail Agent); CPA (Community Postal Agent); CMB (Community Mail Bag) and Mail Service (MS)!
 Tibor Schütz points out many post offices have novelty handling of mail to Santa Claus, even going as far as to allocate special postcodes. For example, in Germany: Santa Claus Nordpolen, Julemandes Postkontor, DK-3900 Nuuk; in Canada: Santa Claus, North Pole, H0H 0H0; in the UK: Father Christmas, Santa’s Grotto, Reindeerland, XM4 5HQ
 Matthieu Valleton got in touch to point out his address on Kerguelen Island ( Google Map), a French territory in the Indian Ocean, his address was District de Kerguelen (island), Terres Australes et Antarctiques Françaises (territory), via la Réunion (indicates where the mail should be routed through), France (country)
 Monty points out that uncommon political hierarchies can lead to uncommon postal addresses. For example, the entire of the Falkland Islands shares postal code FIQQ 1ZZ. On the other hand, the British Virgin Islands have their own postal code system.
 David Kuder pointed me to a 1990 article: Risks Digest correspondent  Tim Kay had problems getting mail sent to his university campus pigeon hole: Timothy L. Kay, Box 256-80, Pasadena, CA 91125. Reportedly automatic systems changed his zip code to 91102.  David Kuder identified this was because all Pasadena PO Boxes were in box 91102.
 Alastair Houghton reminds us the Greek tax office's address is Χανδρή 1 & Θεσσαλονίκης, Τ.Κ. 18346, Αθήνα
 Wikipedia has  a photo of a parcel where a Russian/Cyrillic address was displayed on a computer with the wrong character encoding, and transcribed from that. Reportedly a russian postal worker was able to reverse the mapping and deliver the parcel.
 @shyhoof wrote an poem about an address label with ó converted, via latin1 and two rounds of HTML entities, into &AMPAMP;ATILDE;&AMPAMP;SUP3;
 Alastair Houghton points out addresses may be written in the character set of the source country.
 International mail may specify the country (and possibly other details) in both the source and destination countries' character sets, so it can be read by postal workers on both ends.
 Alastair Houghton provided this example of a Japanese address: 〒100-8994 (zip code), 東京都 (Tokyo-to, i.e. Tokyo prefecture or state) 中央区 (Chuo-ku, i.e. Chuo Ward) 八重洲一丁目 (Yaesu 1-chome, i.e. Yaesu district 1st subdistrict) 5番3号 (block 5 lot 3), 東京中央郵便局 (Tokyo Central Post Office). Thanks to Norman Diamond for telling me which parts of the address are which!
 Erik Engheim and Jan Jongboo
......

业余无线电数字通信(ARDC)总裁证实，他们从亚马逊获得了1.08亿美元的400万个业余无线电TCP/IP地址。
互联网网络44(44.0.0.0/8)名为AMPRnet™，自八十年代中期分配给业余无线电以来，业余无线电操作员一直使用该网络进行科学研究和试验无线电数字通信，目的是提高业余无线电联网的技术水平，并教育业余无线电操作员使用这些技术。
业余无线电数字通信(ARDC)是加州成立的一家非营利性公司，目的是为了实现这些目标。
2019年年中，ARDC将1600万个可用的AMPRnet™IP地址块(44.192.0.0/10)出售给了亚马逊，但直到现在才公布了售价。亚马逊为每个IPv4地址支付了27美元。
“与亚马逊签订的涵盖出售我们剩余IP地址的保密协议要求我们对确切的美元金额保密，直到我们被法律要求在我们的年度纳税申报、审计和财务报告中披露这些金额。这些资料刚刚公之于众，并可通过加州总检察长的网站在线获取(因为ARDC是在加州注册成立的)。您还可以通过我们(独立编写的)维基百科文章获取一些背景信息。底线是，这笔交易为我们带来了约1.08亿美元的净收入，我们预计将根据我们的投资表现，持续*每年*至少拨款500万美元，用于各种互联网和业余无线电数字通信项目。到目前为止，我们已经获得了大约250万美元的赠款，所以我们才刚刚开始。“
note_en:
President of Amateur Radio Digital Communications (ARDC) has confirmed they received $108 million from Amazon for 4 million amateur radio TCP/IP addresses
 Since its allocation to Amateur Radio in the mid-1980s, Internet network 44 (44.0.0.0/8), known as the AMPRNet™, has been used by amateur radio operators to conduct scientific research and to experiment with digital communications over the radio with a goal of advancing the state of the art of Amateur Radio networking, and to educate amateur radio operators in these techniques.
 Amateur Radio Digital Communications (ARDC) is a non-profit California corporation formed to further these goals.
 In mid-2019 a block (44.192.0.0/10) of approximately four million AMPRNet™ IP addresses, out of the 16 million available, was sold to Amazon by ARDC but it is only now that the sale price has been released. Amazon paid $27 for each IPv4 address.
 “The NDA with Amazon that covered the sale of our surplus IP addresses required us to keep the exact dollar amounts private until we were legally required to disclose them in our annual tax filings, audit and financial reports. These have just been made public and are available online through the California Attorney General’s website (since ARDC is incorporated in California). You can also get some background information through our (independently written) Wikipedia article.  The bottom line is that the sale netted us about US $108M, and we expect to grant at least US $5 million *per year* on an ongoing basis, depending on how our investments perform, to a wide variety of Internet and amateur radio digital communication projects. To date we have made about $2.5 million in grants, so we’re only getting started. ”

Manim是用于解释数学视频的动画引擎。它用于以编程方式创建精确的动画，如3Blue1Brown的视频所示。
此存储库包含3Blue1Brown使用的Manim版本。在https://github.com/ManimCommunity/manim/.To上还有一个社区维护的版本，希望获得帮助或加入开发工作，请加入不和谐的行列。
Manim在Python3.6或更高版本上运行。您可以通过pip从PyPI安装它：
系统要求是cairo、ffmpeg、sox(如果要在运行后播放提示音，则为可选)、latex(如果要使用LaTeX，则为可选)。
如果您想要攻击manimlib本身，请克隆此存储库并在目录中执行：
#安装python要求spython3-m pip install-r requirements.txt#尝试outpython3./manim.py示例_Scarees.py SquareToCircle-pl。
安装开罗。对于大多数用户来说，pycairo-1.18.0-cp37-cp37m-win32.whl就可以了(您可以从这里下载它或其他版本)。
由于要将所有依赖项设置得恰到好处有点棘手，因此在此Repo中提供了Dockerfile和Compose文件，并在Docker Hub上提供了预制的图像。Dockerfile包含有关如何构建Manim映像的说明，而Compose文件包含有关如何运行映像的说明。
预构建的容器映像包含Manim存储库。INPUT_PATH是容器查找场景文件的位置。必须将INPUT_PATHEnvironment变量设置为包含场景文件的绝对路径，将OUTPUT_PATH环境变量设置为要写入介质的目录。
如果您的用户名不在扩展坞组中，则需要以root用户身份运行该命令。
运行之后，输出将显示位于/tmp/output/的文件就绪，这指的是容器内的路径。您的output_path被绑定挂载到此/tmp/output，因此容器对/tmp/output所做的任何更改都将镜像到您的output_path上。/media/将在OUTPUT_PATH中创建。
-P赢不了工作，因为Manim会在集装箱系统中寻找视频播放器，而这是它没有的。
当您第一次执行上述命令时，Docker会将镜像从Docker Hub拉取并缓存。在该映像被逐出之前的任何后续运行都将使用缓存的映像。请注意，该映像没有安装任何开发工具，无法预览动画。它的目的只是构建和测试。
上面命令中的-p标志用于预览，这意味着视频文件将在渲染完成后自动打开。-l标志用于以较低质量进行更快的渲染。
设置MEDIA_DIR环境变量以指定图像和动画文件的写入位置。
查看old_jects文件夹，查看之前3b1b视频的代码。但是，请注意，通常对库进行开发时没有考虑与那些旧项目的向后兼容性。要运行一个旧项目并保证它会工作，您必须返回到完成该项目的提交。
在开发场景时，-sp标志有助于只查看结尾的情况，而不必生成完整的动画。使用-n标志跳过一定数量的动画也很有帮助。
文档正在eulerour.com/docs上进行。还有一个由@manim-pytuten维护的一体式文档和教程：manim.ml(中文版)。
Todd Zimmerman整理了一个关于Manim入门的教程，该教程已经更新，可以在Python3.7上运行。
Manim-幼儿园在Manim_sandbox repo中编写并收集了一些有用的课外课程和视频代码。
只接受修复错误/修复打字错误/改进现有内容的拉取请求(有关更多信息，请参阅#1243)。大多数拉取请求应该定向到社区版本。
_3b1b目录中的所有文件基本上都为3b1b视频生成视觉效果，版权所有为3Blue1Brown。
另一方面，在存储库的其余部分中找到的通用动画代码受麻省理工学院的许可。
note_en:
Manim is an animation engine for explanatory math videos. It's used to create precise animations programmatically, as seen in the videos at  3Blue1Brown.
 This repository contains the version of manim used by 3Blue1Brown. There is also a community maintained version at  https://github.com/ManimCommunity/manim/.To get help or to join the development effort, please join the  discord.
  Manim runs on Python 3.6 or higher version. You can install it from PyPI via pip:
  System requirements are  cairo,  ffmpeg,  sox (optional, if you want to play the prompt tone after running),  latex (optional, if you want to use LaTeX).
     If you want to hack on manimlib itself, clone this repository and in that directory execute:
 # Install python requirementspython3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt  # Try it outpython3 ./manim.py example_scenes.py SquareToCircle -pl
  Install Cairo. For most users,  pycairo‑1.18.0‑cp37‑cp37m‑win32.whl will do fine (you can download it or other versions from  here).
       Since it's a bit tricky to get all the dependencies set up just right, there is a Dockerfile and Compose file provided in this repo as well as  a premade image on Docker Hub. The Dockerfile contains instructions on how to build a manim image, while the Compose file contains instructions on how to run the image.
 The prebuilt container image has manim repository included. INPUT_PATH is where the container looks for scene files. You must set the  INPUT_PATHenvironment variable to the absolute path containing your scene file and the OUTPUT_PATH environment variable to the directory where you want media to be written.
   The command needs to be run as root if your username is not in the docker group.
   After running the output will say files ready at  /tmp/output/, which refers to path inside the container. Your  OUTPUT_PATH is bind mounted to this  /tmp/output so any changes made by the container to  /tmp/output will be mirrored on your  OUTPUT_PATH.  /media/ will be created in  OUTPUT_PATH.
 -p won't work as manim would look for video player in the container system, which it does not have.
 The first time you execute the above command, Docker will pull the image from Docker Hub and cache it. Any subsequent runs until the image is evicted will use the cached image.Note that the image doesn't have any development tools installed and can't preview animations. Its purpose is building and testing only.
    The  -p flag in the command above is for previewing, meaning the video file will automatically open when it is done rendering. The  -l flag is for a faster rendering at a lower quality.
   Set  MEDIA_DIR environment variable to specify where the image and animation files will be written.
 Look through the  old_projects folder to see the code for previous 3b1b videos. Note, however, that developments are often made to the library without considering backwards compatibility with those old projects. To run an old project with a guarantee that it will work, you will have to go back to the commit which completed that project.
 While developing a scene, the  -sp flags are helpful to just see what things look like at the end without having to generate the full animation. It can also be helpful to use the  -n flag to skip over some number of animations.
  Documentation is in progress at  eulertour.com/docs. And there is also an all-in-one documentation and tutorials maintained by  @manim-kindergarten:  manim.ml (in Chinese).
  Todd Zimmerman put together a  tutorial on getting started with manim, which has been updated to run on Python 3.7.
 manim-kindergarten wrote and collected some useful extra classes and some codes of videos in  manim_sandbox repo.
  Only accepts pull requests that fixes bugs / fixes typos / improves existing content (for more information, see  #1243). Most pull requests should be directed to the  community version.
  All files in the directory  from_3b1b, which by and large generate the visuals for 3b1b videos, are copyright 3Blue1Brown.
 The general purpose animation code found in the remainder of the repository, on the other hand, is under the MIT license.
title: Manim：用于解释数学视频的动画引擎

note:
可以说我傲慢，但我宁愿优化我的室内环境，也不愿花更多的时间在反复无常的户外。我认为，放弃改善我们的室内空间，听天由命于变化无常的天气和季节，是一种失败主义。
如果我要为人类创造一个理想的环境，我想有几样东西我们通常不会包括在我们的家庭和办公室里。
我们的室内照明情况通常很糟糕。“自然采光”是房地产的一个卖点，这一事实表明我们在这个部门的工作是多么糟糕。我们依靠太阳自然地提供充足的光线，如果是阴天或者冬天白天变短了，那么我想我们就他妈的运气不佳了。
通常，室内区域约为50-500勒克斯。这比阳光暗几百倍。显然，我们并不是被设计成在如此昏暗的环境中茁壮成长的，科学确实证实了更明亮的光线和警觉之间的联系。如果我们不想像夜里一样困倦，我们就不应该像夜里一样照亮我们的房间。对于一些人来说，昏暗的灯光造成的影响不仅仅是简单的昏昏欲睡，特别是在冬天，会导致严重的情绪问题，如季节性情感障碍或冬季忧郁。这很常见，但不是必须的。明亮的光线，特别是蓝光，通常也能提振情绪，可能是与咖啡因相当的兴奋剂。(那些容易患躁狂症的人应该小心，因为强光可能会在那些有躁狂症倾向的人中引发躁狂症或轻度躁狂症。)。更明亮的灯光也可以帮助解决昼夜节律问题(例如，我多年来一直在努力解决这一问题)，这既可以通过调节你的昼夜节律，让你的身体更好地知道什么时候是白天，也可以在昼夜太长时缩短昼夜节律。
照明不像以前那么贵了，所以我们可以做得比过去更好。LED照明的电力成本现在可以忽略不计，唯一真正的因素是灯泡本身的成本。达到100,000勒克斯的阳光仍然是令人望而却步的昂贵，但至少10,000勒克斯是可行的，只需几百美元。我不会在此详述，但您可以在此处或此处获得有关特定照明设置的更多信息。特别要选择色温接近阳光(5600K)的灯泡，但要确保灯泡具有良好的90+显色指数(CRI)，否则光线会很刺眼。
我建议你给家里买的任何明亮的灯都装上电子定时器，这样你就不会在晚上不小心让它们亮着，搞砸了你的睡眠。如果可以，您可能还想将手机/电脑亮度设置为计时器。我们的目标是模仿我们进化环境的自然昼夜循环，但不会出现所有令人讨厌的自然波动。你也可以安装像f.lux这样的程序，它可以减少你的设备在晚上发出的蓝光，但根据我的经验，这还不够好，降低设备在晚上的实际亮度也很重要。
“但是维生素D呢？你就到外面去吧！“。这是个糟糕的建议，我听得太多了。阳光是一种强烈的致癌物质，而维生素D补充剂不是，而且它们很便宜。
一氧化碳是一种致命的一氧化碳，你可能已经在家里安装了一台监视器。二氧化二碳是一氧化碳的微弱表亲，但它仍然对人类健康有负面影响：高(但常见的)1000ppm或更高的水平会削弱我们的思考能力。这正是你在办公室里不想要的。高水平还可能对我们健康的其他领域产生负面的长期影响。
屏住呼吸。当它很糟糕，你决定重新开始呼吸时，是二氧化碳积聚，而不是缺氧，导致你感到恐慌，需要呼吸。二氧化碳是一种毒素。我们把它呼出到通风不良的房间里，那里的空气浓度可能会上升到大约400ppm的室外水平的两到三倍。
几项研究表明，二氧化碳水平超过1000ppm会造成严重的(暂时性)认知障碍，但这样的水平很常见。我最近买了一个二氧化碳测量仪，在家里发现了这样的水平。在通风不良的共享空间里。幸运的是，解决方案很简单：打开一扇窗。不幸的是，在下雨、外面太热或…外面太冷的时候，这是不起作用的。特别是，我必须与加拿大的冬天作斗争，这意味着对于一年中的少数人来说，打开窗户是一个有效的策略，除非我买了一台昂贵的热回收通风机。我没有一个很好的解决方案来减轻冬天的二氧化碳积聚。如果你知道了就告诉我。
顺便说一句，植物不会起作用。它们不会吸收足够的二氧化碳。每个人需要数百株植物，或者说大约12棵全尺寸的树木，才能抵消人类在一个房间里呼出的二氧化碳。
一个有趣的事实是：如果我们不停止向大气中排放二氧化碳，那么大约一个世纪后，户外的二氧化碳可能会达到损害认知的水平。那我们该怎么办？
高/低湿度和高/低温度都会导致不适，集中测量得分较低。人们一般都能控制住温度，或者至少是他们意识到的东西。湿度不太常见，但是10美元的湿度计可以帮助你把室内空间调到理想的30%-50%的湿度范围(如果还没有的话)。空调也往往会降低湿度和温度，所以夏天开空调，冬天用加湿器。
在晚上，如果可以的话，把温度降低几度；在凉爽的房间里睡觉会更容易。我想知道，由于智能恒温器的出现，已经收回了多少小时的睡眠时间。
我想这个因素比其他因素更主观，但声音太大会让人分心，甚至令人愤慨；太安静会让别人痛苦地听到你的抽泣和叹息，也会让人分心。交通等不均匀背景噪声比白噪声或涓涓流水的均匀背景噪声要差。糟糕的背景噪声会导致认知能力和注意力的下降。
很容易被噪音打扰，直到噪音停止，一阵解脱的浪潮终于让你意识到你对噪音有多恼火，你才意识到这一点。令人高兴的是，噪音问题很容易控制：耳塞或降噪耳机通常会起到作用。完全消除环境中烦人的噪音是理所当然的，但这是没有必要的。
一位通常在车里服药的海洛因成瘾者决定有一天在他们的浴室里注射。他们死于服药过量，尽管他们服用的量与平时相同。为什么？我们的大脑与不同的环境保持联系。如果你通常在上车时注射海洛因，那么你的身体一上车就会开始为你注射毒品做准备。因此，药物耐受性在一定程度上是由环境因素造成的。(这实际上发生了，而且经常发生。)。由于巴甫洛夫式的条件反射，你的思想和身体都会受到你所处环境的影响。铃声一响，狗就流口水。当午餐铃声响起的时候，你也是。
医生给失眠症患者的一个常见建议是，只使用你的床睡觉和做爱，这是一个很好的建议。如果你把床用来读书、学习和看电视，那么你的大脑就不会把床和睡眠联系起来，你会更难入睡。
同样，如果你在同一张办公桌上做所有的懒散工作，你可能会更难集中精力。即使在你工作的时候把你的智能手机放在你的视野内，也被证明会减少焦点。因此，有不同的区域用于工作和娱乐，不在办公桌上吃饭也不会有什么坏处。(甚至是工作和非工作的不同用户帐户，如果你不觉得这个想法像我一样令人头疼的话。)。
我们不仅与空间有联系，而且与时间有联系。因此，另一条常见的睡眠卫生学建议是：每晚在同一时间睡觉。你的身体会在那个时候学会期待睡眠。同样，每天在同一时间进食的人在身体准备好接受食物的情况下进食，因此变得肥胖的可能性较小。研究表明这一点。不幸的是，将你生活的方方面面都设定在时钟上会让你感觉自己像个机器人，所以我通常不能容忍生活中的这种僵化。但这是值得考虑的。
我也认为我们大多数室内环境的美学需要升级，但我在这个问题上没有太多要说的，除了简单地这么说。(不过我敢打赌：绿色、郁郁葱葱&灰色单调。)。
我们有时表现得就像是⟶代码中的咖啡因机器，但我们不是。我们是软绵绵的生物，有着脆弱的身体和脆弱的心灵。我们为一种特定的环境进化。不能保证生产成本最低的室内环境会像定制的对我们进化环境的模仿一样对我们有好处，事实上并非如此。我认为，如果人们在设计室内环境时更认真地对待这些因素，生活会更愉快，我们的工作也会更有效率，更不容易出错。
note_en:
Call me arrogant, but I’d rather optimize my indoor environment than try to spend more time in the capricious outdoors. I think it’s defeatism to give up on improving our indoor spaces and resign ourselves to the fickle weather and seasons.
 If I was going to create an ideal environment for a human, I think there are several things I would include that we routinely fail to include in our homes and offices.
  Our indoor lighting situation usually sucks. The fact that “natural lighting” is a selling point in real estate shows how terrible a job we are doing in this department. We rely on the sun naturally providing us with sufficient light, and if it’s an overcast day or the days have grown shorter in the winter, then I guess we’re shit out of luck.
 Usually, indoor areas are around 50-500 lux. This is hundreds of times dimmer than the sunlight. Clearly, we weren’t designed to thrive in such dim environments, and science does verify a connection between brighter light and alertness. If we don’t want to be sleepy like it’s nighttime, we shouldn’t light our rooms like it’s nighttime. For some, the effects of dim lighting go beyond simple lethargy and, especially in the winter, cause serious mood problems like seasonal affective disorder or the winter blues. This is common, but it’s not necessary. Bright light, particularly blue light, can also generally boost mood and may be a comparable stimulant to caffeine. (Those who are prone to mania should be careful, as intense light can trigger mania or hypomania in those predisposed.) Brighter lighting can also help circadian rhythm issues (which I, for example, have struggled with for years), both by entraining your circadian rhythm so your body better knows when it’s day, and by shortening it if it’s too long.
 Lighting isn’t as expensive as it used to be, so we can do better than we have in the past. The cost of electricity for LED lighting is now negligible, and the only real factor is the cost of the bulbs themselves. Reaching for the full 100,000 lux of sunlight would still be prohibitively expensive, but going for at least 10,000 lux is doable with only a few hundred dollars. I won’t go into specifics here, but you can get more information on specific lighting setups  here or  here. In particular, get bulbs with a color temperature close to sunlight (5600k), but make sure the bulbs have a  good good means 90+ Color Rendering Index (CRI), otherwise the light will feel harsh.
 I recommend putting any bright lighting you buy for your home on electrical timers so you don’t accidentally leave them on during the evening and screw up your sleep. You may also want to set your phone/computer brightness on a timer, if you can. The goal is to mimic the natural day/night cycle of our evolutionary environment, but without all the pesky volatility of nature. You can get programs like f.lux too, which reduce the amount of blue light emitted by your device in the evening, but in my experience this isn’t good enough and reducing the actual brightness of the device at night is also important.
 “But what about vitamin D? Just go outside!” This is terrible advice, and I hear it too often. Sunlight is a powerful carcinogen, and vitamin D supplements are not, and they’re cheap.
  Carbon MON-oxide is the deadly one you probably already have a monitor for in your house. Carbon DI-oxide is the feeble cousin of carbon monoxide, but it still has a negative effect on human health:  high (but common) 1,000 ppm or higher levels impairs our ability to think. Just what you don’t want in an office. High levels may also have a negative long-term impact in other areas of our health.
 Hold your breath. When it sucks and you decide to start breathing again, it’s carbon dioxide buildup, not lack of oxygen, causing you to feel panic and the need to breath. Carbon dioxide is a toxin. And we breath it out into poorly ventilated rooms, where the levels can rise to double or triple what they are  outdoors around 400 ppm.
 Several studies have shown significant (temporary) cognitive impairments due to carbon dioxide levels over 1,000 ppm, but such levels are  common I recently bought a carbon dioxide meter and found such levels in my home. in poorly ventilated shared spaces. Fortunately, the solution is simple: open a window. Unfortunately, this doesn’t work when it’s raining, or when it’s too hot outside, or when it’s too cold outside… In particular, I have to contend with Canadian winters, which means opening the window is a valid strategy for a minority of the year unless I buy an expensive heat recovery ventilator. I don’t have a good solution for mitigating carbon dioxide buildup in the winter. Let me know if you do.
 And, by the way, plants won’t work. They won’t suck up nearly enough carbon dioxide. You would need hundreds of plants per person, or roughly a dozen full-size trees per person, to offset the carbon dioxide exhaled by humans in a room.
 A fun fact: if we don’t stop pumping carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, then in about a century, carbon dioxide  outdoors may reach cognitively impairing levels. Then what do we do?
  High/low humidity and high/low temperature both lead to discomfort and lower scores on concentration measures. People generally have temperature under control, or at least it’s something they’re aware of. Humidity is less common to measure, but a $10 hygrometer should help you get your indoor space to the ideal 30-50% humidity range if it isn’t already. Air conditioners also tend to reduce humidity as well as temperature, so air-condition in the summer and use a humidifier in the winter.
 At night, drop the temperature a few degrees if you can; It’s easier to sleep in a cool room. I wonder how many hours of sleep have been reclaimed already due to the advent of smart thermostats.
  I imagine this factor is more subjective than the others, but too loud is distracting, even aggrivating; too quiet makes your sniffles and sighs painfully audible to others, and so is distracting. Uneven background noise like traffic is worse than the uniform background noise of white noise or trickling water. Bad background noise leads to poorer cognition and focus.
 It’s easy to be bothered by noise and not realize it until the noise stops and a wave of relief finally makes you aware of how annoyed you were by the sound. Noise issues are happily easy to control: earplugs or noise-cancelling headphones will generally do the trick. It would be utopic to eliminate bothersome noise from the environment altogether, but it’s not necessary.
  A heroin addict who normally takes their dose in their car decides one day to inject in their bathroom. They die of an overdose, even though they took the same amount they normally do. Why? Our brains maintain associations with different environments. If you normally inject heroin when you get in your car, then your body starts to prepare you for the drug as soon as you get in the car. Drug tolerance, then, is partly environmental. (This  actually happened and happens regularly.) Your mind and body are affected by your environment due to Pavlovian conditioning. When the bell rings, the dog salivates. When the lunch bell rings, so do you.
 One common piece of advice given by doctors to insomniacs is to only use your bed for sleeping and for sex, and it’s good advice. If you use your bed for reading, studying, and watching TV, then your mind will not form a strong association between the bed and sleep, and you will have a harder time falling asleep.
 Likewise, if you do all your slacking off at the same desk you do your work at, you will probably have a harder time focusing. Even having your smartphone within your field of view while you work has been shown to reduce focus. So it wouldn’t hurt to have different areas for work and play, and to not eat at your desk. (And even different user accounts on your computer for work and non-work, if you don’t find that idea to be a pain in the ass like I do.)
 We also form associations not just with space, but with time. Hence another piece of common sleep hygeine advice: go to sleep at the same time every night. Your body will learn to expect sleep at that time. Likewise, people who eat at the same time every day eat with their bodies prepared to receive food, and so are less likely to become obese. Studies have shown this. Unfortunately, setting every aspect of your life to a clock can make you feel like a robot, so I usually don’t tolerate such rigidity in my life. But it’s worth thinking about.
   I also think the aesthetics of most of our indoor environments could use an upgrade, but I don’t have much to say on the subject besides simply saying so. (Though I would bet: green lush > grey drab.)
 We sometimes act like we are just  machines caffeine in ⟶ code out, but we are not. We’re mushy creatures with delicate bodies and delicate minds, too. And we evolved for one specific environment. There is no guarantee that the indoor environment which is cheapest to produce is going to be just as good for us as a bespoke imitation of our evolutionary environment, and in fact it is not. I think life would be more pleasant if people took these factors more serously when designing indoor environments, and our work would be more efficient and less prone to mistakes.

P是一种用于异步事件驱动编程的语言。P允许程序员将系统指定为交互状态机的集合，这些状态机使用事件相互通信。P将建模和编程统一到程序员的一项活动中。不仅可以将P程序编译成可执行代码，而且还可以使用模型检查对其进行系统测试。P已用于实现和验证Microsoft Windows 8和Windows Phone附带的USB设备驱动程序堆栈。目前，p在亚马逊(Amazon，AWS)内部被广泛用于复杂分布式系统的模型检查。
“动态分布式系统的组合编程和测试”，Ankush Desai，Amar Phanishayee，Shaz Qadeer，and Sanjit Seshi.面向对象编程，系统，语言，和应用程序国际会议(OOPSLA)，2018。
“Drona：一种安全的分布式移动机器人框架”，Ankush Desai，Indranil Saha，杨建桥，Shaz Qadeer，Sanjit A.Seshi.在第八届ACM/IEEE国际网络物理系统会议(ICCPS)上，2017.。
“异步反应式系统的系统测试”，Ankush Desai，Shaz Qadeer，and Sanjit A.Seshi.在2015年第10届软件工程基础联席会议(ESEC/FSE 2015)论文集上.。
“安全异步事件驱动编程”，Ankush Desai，Vivek Gupta，Ethan Jackson，Shaz Qadeer，Sriram Rajamani，和Damien Zuffere.ACM SIGPLAN Conference on Programming Language Design and Implementation(PLDI)，2013。
观看有趣的演示视频，使用P控制四轴飞行器并理解MavLink流，所有这些都在实时DGML图表中可视化。

note_en:
P is a language for asynchronous event-driven programming. P allows the programmer to specify the system as a collection of interacting state machines, which communicate with each other using events. P unifies modeling and programming into one activity for the programmer. Not only can a P program be compiled into executable code, but it can also be systematically tested using Model Checking. P has been used to implement and validate the USB device driver stack that ships with Microsoft Windows 8 and Windows Phone. P is currently being used extensively inside Amazon (AWS) for model checking complex distributed systems.
  Compositional Programming and Testing of Dynamic Distributed Systems.Ankush Desai, Amar Phanishayee, Shaz Qadeer, and Sanjit Seshia.International Conference on Object-Oriented Programming, Systems, Languages, and Applications (OOPSLA), 2018.
 DRONA: A Framework for Safe Distributed Mobile Robotics.Ankush Desai, Indranil Saha, Jianqiao Yang, Shaz Qadeer, and Sanjit A. Seshia.In Proceedings of the 8th ACM/IEEE International Conference on Cyber-Physical Systems (ICCPS), 2017.
 Systematic Testing of Asynchronous Reactive Systems.Ankush Desai, Shaz Qadeer, and Sanjit A. Seshia.In Proceedings of the 2015 10th Joint Meeting on Foundations of Software Engineering (ESEC/FSE 2015).
 P: Safe asynchronous event-driven programming.Ankush Desai, Vivek Gupta, Ethan Jackson, Shaz Qadeer, Sriram Rajamani, and Damien Zufferey.In Proceedings of ACM SIGPLAN Conference on Programming Language Design and Implementation (PLDI), 2013.
     See  fun demo video using P to control a quadrocopter and make sense of the MavLink stream, all visualized in a live DGML diagram.
  We built DRONA, a software framework for distributed mobile robotics systems. DRONA uses P language for implementing and model-checking the distributed robotics software stack (  wiki). The C code generated from P compiler can be easily deployed on Robot Operating System (ROS).More details about the DRONA framework and simulation videos are available here:  https://drona-org.github.io/Drona/
title: P：一种用于异步事件驱动编程和模型检查的语言

Work&Amp；公司几十年来首次对地铁地图进行了重大重新设计，备受赞誉的电影制作人加里·赫斯特威特(Gary Hustwit)记录了创建新的实时地图的过程，该地图可以实时更新，以帮助纽约人和游客更好地计划他们的旅行。
实时地铁地图可以在map.mta.info上找到，对于打印的地图和车站墙上常用的地图来说，它是一个更准确、更具技术先进性的选择。使用MTA自己的数据，这张地图基于网络，无需下载即可通过多种设备访问，不断更新路线，以说明当前准确的列车服务状态。
在发布新地图之前，Work&amp；Co、MTA和公共交通创新伙伴关系(Transportation Innovation Partnership)建立了18个月的合作伙伴关系。公共交通创新伙伴关系是MTA和纽约市合作伙伴关系的公私合作伙伴关系。Work&amp；Co主导设计概念、详细设计、技术架构、Web应用程序开发和质量保证。
Work&amp；Co与Hustwit合作制作了一部名为“地图”的短片，并展示了好的设计和最新的数字技术如何简化了世界上最复杂的交通系统之一。Hustwit是设计纪录片“Helvetica”、“Objectified”和“Rams”的导演。点击这里观看视频。
Work&amp；Co.的创始合伙人费利佩·马莫里亚说：“我们看到了打造适合我们这个时代的工具来帮助纽约市的机会。”作为设计师，我们钦佩MTA传奇印刷地图的历史，但技术使我们能够创造出更强大的东西。我们正在为世界各地的交通系统采用实时地图奠定基础，以进一步鼓励人们使用公共交通工具。
当我们接近最初预计的发射时，新冠肺炎震惊了世界，纽约市受到的打击尤其严重。我们一直在开发的产品具有了新的意义-说明了准确的列车时间数据不仅对于管理通勤至关重要，而且可以帮助乘客计划更高效的乘车和在公共场所的有限时间。
纽约市公共交通临时总裁莎拉·范伯格(Sarah Feinberg)指出，这张地图是“我们正在采取的各种努力的一部分，目的是为我们的客户提供工具，以帮助确保他们的旅行尽可能容易和可靠。”她补充说：“尤其是在交通局因新冠肺炎而面临前所未有的财政危机之际，我要感谢Work&Amp；Co为交通系统和所有纽约人做出的令人难以置信的贡献。”
自动更新列车线路：列车线路将使用实时数据重新绘制，以显示当前准确的列车服务状态。列车线路的部分在列车线路不运行的地方淡出，如果列车是单向运行的，则用虚线表示。
移动中的列车：用户将看到列车在移动，这有助于向用户发出信号，表明地图是实时的，并且还反映了整个地铁系统中列车的实时位置。
放大功能：当用户放大时，会显示更多的地图详细信息，包括能够查看单独的列车线路、地铁入口、车站名称以及街道位置和名称。
地铁无障碍：新地图突出了无障碍车站，并提供了与无障碍相关的设备(如电梯和自动扶梯)的更新。
紧急警报：地图使用MTA的数据馈送来传达MTA在紧急情况下的官方通信。
Work&amp；Co每年都会要求团队成员确定他们可能希望从事的对社会做出贡献和改善的项目，帮助MTA更新其标志性地图--以及改善和鼓励使用公共交通--被列为优先事项。这是一个公益项目。该公司推出的其他旨在帮助社会的公益数字产品包括计划生育的Roo、GiveBlck.org，以及最近的Wake Vote。

note_en:
Work & Co has launched the   first major redesign of the MTA subway map in decades, and acclaimed filmmaker Gary Hustwit documented the process of creating a new "live map" — one that updates in real-time — to help New Yorkers and tourists better plan their journeys.
 The Live Subway Map, which can be found at   map.mta.info, is a more accurate, tech-forward option to printed maps and those commonly used on station walls. Using the MTA’s own data, the map, which is web-based to reach across a multitude of devices without a download, continually updates routes to illustrate current and accurate train service status.
 The launch of the new map follows an 18-month partnership between Work & Co, the MTA, and the Transit Innovation Partnership, a public-private partnership between the MTA and the partnership for New York City. Work & Co led design concepting, detailed design, technical architecture, web application development, and quality assurance.
 Work & Co collaborated with Hustwit —the director of design documentaries, including Helvetica, Objectified, and Rams— to produce a short film titled "The Map." The film examines the evolution of wayfinding and user interfaces and shows how good design and the latest digital technology can simplify one of the world's most complex transit systems. View the video   up here.
 "We saw an opportunity to help New York City by building a tool appropriate for our time,” said Felipe Memoria, founding partner at Work & Co. "As designers, we admire the history of the MTA’s legendary printed maps, but technology enables us to create something more powerful. We are laying the foundation for transit systems around the world to adopt real-time maps that further encourage the use of public transportation."
 As we neared our original estimated launch, COVID-19 hit the world, and New York City was hit especially hard. The product we had been working on took on new meaning — illustrating how accurate train time data is not only critical for managing commutes but can help riders plan for more efficient riding and limited time in public places.
 Sarah Feinberg, Interim President of New York City Transit, noted that the map is “part of a variety of efforts we’re taking to provide our customers with tools to help ensure their trips are as easy and dependable as possible.” She added: Especially valuable at a time when the MTA is facing a fiscal crisis of unprecedented magnitude because of COVID-19, I want to thank Work & Co for this incredible contribution to the transit system and to all New Yorkers.”
  Automatically updating train lines: Train lines will redraw themselves using real-time data to illustrate current and accurate train service status. Sections of train lines fade out where a train line is not running and are denoted with dashes if trains are running in a single direction.
 Moving trains: The user will see trains moving, which helps to signal to users that the map is live and also reflect real-time locations of trains throughout the subway system.
 Zoom-In features: Greater map detail is exposed as the user zooms in, including the ability to see individual train lines, subway entrances, station names, and street locations and names.
 Subway accessibility: The new map highlights accessible stations and provides updates to accessibility-related equipment like elevators and escalators.
 Emergency alerts: The map uses the MTA’s data feed to convey official MTA communications for emergencies.
 Work & Co on an annual basis asks team members to identify projects they may want to work on that contribute to and improve society, and helping the MTA update its iconic map —and improve and encourage the use of public transport—came up as a priority. This was a pro-bono project. Other pro-bono digital products the company has launched with the aim of helping society have included   Planned Parenthood’s Roo,   GiveBlck.org, and, most recently,   Woke Vote.
 Read more about our partnership with the MTA in   The New York Times,   Curbed,   Fast Company,   Time Out New York, and   Gothamist.

冠状病毒通过空气传播，特别是在室内空间。虽然它不像麻疹那样具有传染性，但科学家现在公开承认气溶胶传播所起的作用。气溶胶是感染者呼出的微小传染性颗粒，仍然悬浮在室内环境的空气中。变速箱是怎么工作的？更重要的是，我们如何才能阻止它？

目前，卫生当局确认了三种冠状病毒的传播途径：说话或咳嗽时产生的小飞沫，最终可能进入站在附近人的眼睛、嘴巴或鼻子里；被污染的表面(杂物)，尽管美国疾病控制和预防中心表示这是感染病毒的最不可能的途径，但得到欧洲疾病控制和预防中心观察支持的结论是，尚未观察到一例由杂音引起的新冠肺炎病例。最后，还有气溶胶传播--被感染者呼出的看不见的传染性颗粒吸入，一旦离开口腔，其行为方式与吸烟相似。在没有通风的情况下，气溶胶仍然悬浮在空气中，并随着时间的推移变得越来越稠密。
在没有通风的情况下，气溶胶仍然悬浮在空气中，随着时间的推移变得越来越集中。
在大流行之初，人们认为我们咳嗽或打喷嚏时排出的大飞沫是主要的传播媒介。但我们现在知道，在室内、通风不良的空间里长时间叫喊和唱歌也会增加传染的风险。这是因为大声说话比我们完全不说话时释放的病毒颗粒多50倍。这些气溶胶如果不通过通风扩散，就会变得越来越集中，这就增加了感染的风险。科学家们已经证明，这些颗粒物-我们在简单呼吸时也会释放到大气中，可以从不适当佩戴的口罩中逃脱-可以感染在感染者半径五米范围内停留几分钟以上的人，这取决于时间的长短和相互作用的性质。在下面的例子中，我们概述了在这种情况下哪些条件会增加传染风险。
在最坏的情况下-在封闭的空间里喊叫或唱歌一个小时-一个和新冠肺炎一起的人释放出1,500个传染性剂量。
在最坏的情况下-在封闭的空间里喊叫或唱歌一个小时-一个和新冠肺炎一起的人释放出1,500个传染性剂量。
在最坏的情况下-在封闭的空间里喊叫或唱歌一个小时-一个和新冠肺炎一起的人释放出1,500个传染性剂量。
今年春天，卫生当局未能关注气溶胶传播，但最近的科学出版物迫使世界卫生组织(WHO)和疾控中心承认这一点。著名的“科学”杂志上的一篇文章发现，有“压倒性的证据”表明，空气传播是冠状病毒的“主要传播途径”，疾控中心现在指出，“在某些情况下，他们似乎已经感染了6英尺(2米)外的其他人。”这些传播发生在通风不足的封闭空间内。例如，有时感染者在唱歌或锻炼时呼吸急促。“。
在活动以及酒吧和餐馆等场所爆发的冠状病毒是社会环境中重要数量的传染病。更重要的是，它们是最具爆炸性的：每一次在夜总会爆发的疫情平均感染27人，而在家庭聚会期间只有6人感染-正如第一张图表中所解释的那样。其中一次超级蔓延的疫情发生在西班牙南部城市科尔多瓦的一家俱乐部，那里有73人在一晚外出后检测呈阳性。科学家最近还分析了越南一家酒吧爆发的疫情，那里有12名顾客感染了病毒。
在西班牙卫生当局记录的冠状病毒疫情中，学校仅占6%。在教室里通过气溶胶传播的动态完全取决于感染者-或零号患者-是学生还是教师。教师的发言远远多于学生，并提高了他们的声音，这就成倍地增加了潜在传染性粒子的排泄。相比之下，受感染的学生只会偶尔说话。根据西班牙国家研究委员会(CSIC)的指导方针，西班牙政府建议教室通风-尽管这可能会在较冷的月份造成不适-或者使用通风设备。
为了计算处于“危险”状态的人之间传播的可能性，我们使用了科罗拉多大学何塞·路易斯·吉梅内斯教授领导的一组科学家开发的Covid空中传播估计器。该工具旨在强调阻碍气溶胶传播的措施的重要性。这个计算不是详尽的，也没有涵盖所有可能影响传播的无数变量，但它有助于说明如何通过改变我们确实可以控制的条件来降低传染的风险。
在模拟过程中，受试者保持推荐的安全距离，消除了通过飞沫传播的风险。但如果不同时应用所有可能的预防措施：正确的通风、缩短接触时间、减少参与者数量和戴口罩，他们仍然可能被感染。理想的情况是，无论在什么情况下，都是在户外，在那里传染的颗粒会迅速扩散。如果不能保持与感染者的安全距离，传播的概率就会成倍增加，因为不仅是气溶胶，还会有飞沫传染的风险。更糟糕的是，即使有通风，如果两个人靠得很近，也不足以驱散气溶胶。
这三种不同情景中显示的计算是基于对气溶胶传播如何发生的研究，使用的是经过详细分析的真实爆发。关于理解室内传播的动态，一个非常中肯的案例是3月份在美国华盛顿州的一次合唱团彩排。120名合唱团成员中只有61人参加了彩排，并努力保持安全距离和卫生措施。但他们并不知道，他们处于最危险的情况下：没有面具，没有通风，长时间唱歌和分享空间。在两个半小时的时间里，只有一名感染者感染了53人。一些感染者在14米外，所以只有气溶胶才能解释这种传播。感染病毒的人中有两人死亡。
在仔细研究了这次暴发后，科学家们能够计算出，如果他们采取措施防止空中传播，风险可以在多大程度上得到缓解。例如，如果戴了口罩，风险就会减半，在场的人中只有44%左右会受到影响，而不是87%。如果彩排在一个通风较多的空间进行较短的时间，只会有两名歌手受到感染。这些超级蔓延的情景似乎越来越对大流行的发展和传播至关重要，这意味着拥有在此类活动中防止大规模传播的工具是控制它的关键。
方法：我们使用了科罗拉多大学大气化学家、空气颗粒物化学和动力学专家何塞·路易斯·吉梅内斯(JoséLuis Jiménez)开发的工具，计算了新冠肺炎感染的风险。世界各地的科学家已经审查了这个估计器，它基于公布的方法和数据来估计感染情景中涉及的不同可测量因素的重要性。然而，Estimator的准确性是有限的，因为它依赖于仍然不确定的数字-例如，描述一个感染者排放了多少传染性病毒的数字。估计者假设人们实行两米社交距离规则，没有人能幸免。我们的计算是基于一般人群的默认值，其中包括广泛的口罩(外科口罩和布料)和大声的声音，这会增加气溶胶的排出量。

The coronavirus is spread through the air, especially in indoor spaces. While it is not as infectious as measles, scientists now openly acknowledge the role played by the transmission of aerosols – tiny contagious particles exhaled by an infected person that remain suspended in the air of an indoor environment. How does the transmission work? And, more importantly, how can we stop it?
 These are respiratory droplets that are less than 100 micrometers in diameter that  can remain suspended in the air for hours
            These are particles that are larger than 300 micrometers and, due to air currents,  fall to the ground in seconds
